| **Shot noise** | Discrete nature of charge carrier flow across a junction | White | Use low-bias-current op-amps, JFET inputs |
| **Flicker (1/f) noise** | Surface traps, contact irregularities | Dominant below **100 Hz** | Chopper-stabilized amplifiers, AC coupling at higher frequencies |
| **Popcorn noise** | Manufacturing defects in semiconductor junctions | Random bursts | Component selection |

### 2.1.3 Environmental Interference
- **50/60 Hz power-line interference** (dominant in clinical settings).
- **RF interference** from electrosurgical units, mobile phones.
- **Electrostatic coupling** from nearby ungrounded conductors.
- **Electromagnetic induction** (time-varying magnetic fields from transformers).

### 2.1.4 Motion Artifacts
- Caused by **mechanical disturbance of the electrode–electrolyte interface**.
- Produces **low-frequency (1–10 Hz) baseline wander**.
- **The hardest artifact to remove digitally** because it overlaps the physiological bandwidth.

## 2.2 The Skin–Electrode Interface Equivalent Circuit

The skin–electrode interface is the **single most important site of motion artifact generation**. Its small-signal model is:

$$R_s \;\text{(series resistance of gel/electrolyte)} \quad \parallel \quad C_d \;\text{(double-layer capacitance)} \quad \text{in series with} \quad E_{hc} \;\text{(half-cell potential)}$$

Where:
- $R_s$ = spread resistance of the electrolyte gel (typically **1–50 kΩ**).
- $C_d$ = double-layer capacitance at the metal–electrolyte boundary (typically **0.1–10 µF/cm²**).
- $E_{hc}$ = half-cell potential (approximately **±0.8 V** for Ag/AgCl, but the **difference** between two electrodes is what matters; ideally **< 50 mV**).

When the electrode moves mechanically, the **effective area of contact changes**, which:
1. Modulates $R_s$ and $C_d$ — producing a varying impedance.
2. Disturbs the electrochemical equilibrium — producing a varying $E_{hc}$.

Both effects appear as an unwanted voltage that is **indistinguishable from the true bioelectric signal** at the input of the amplifier.

## 2.3 Monopolar Configuration — Operational Analysis

### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Operation
1. **Active electrode (A)** is placed over the source of interest (e.g., V1 chest lead in ECG).
2. **Reference electrode (R)** is placed at a site with minimal bioelectric activity (e.g., Wilson's central terminal, right leg).
3. The differential amplifier measures $V_{out} = A \cdot (V_A - V_R) + \text{noise}$.
4. Any interference present in the environment is **NOT cancelled** at this stage because only one active electrode picks it up.

### 2.3.2 Strengths and Limitations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monopolar Strengths:** Simple topology, captures the **absolute potential** at the active site, useful for surface mapping and EEG montages that require a true reference.
>
> **Monopolar Limitations:** Highly susceptible to **common-mode interference**, requires a **very stable reference**, and amplifies any motion artifact generated at the single active electrode.

## 2.4 Bipolar Configuration — Operational Analysis

### 2.4.1 Step-by-Step Operation
1. **Two active electrodes (A and B)** are placed near the source of interest.
2. **Common-mode interference** $V_{cm}$ (e.g., 50 Hz hum) is induced in **both** electrodes with nearly equal amplitude and phase.
3. The **differential amplifier** computes $V_{out} = A \cdot (V_A - V_B)$.
4. The common-mode component is suppressed by a factor equal to the **CMRR**.
5. The **driven-right-leg (DRL) circuit** actively drives the patient's body to minimize $V_{cm}$ at its source.

### 2.4.2 Strengths and Limitations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bipolar Strengths:** Excellent rejection of environmental interference via CMRR, reduced motion artifact because both electrodes move similarly (common-mode), used in **standard clinical ECG, EMG, and EEG** recordings.
>
> **Bipolar Limitations:** Loses the absolute reference potential, requires **well-matched electrode impedances** (difference **< 5 kΩ** at 50 Hz) for the CMRR to be effective.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Variables and units are listed in the right column.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | **Johnson (thermal) noise voltage** | $V_n$ in V; $k_B$ = **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**; $T$ in K; $R$ in Ω; $\Delta f$ in Hz |
| 2 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (dB)** | $A_d$ = differential gain; $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| 3 | $V_{out} = A_d(V_1 - V_2) + \dfrac{A_{cm}(V_1 + V_2)}{2}$ | **Differential amplifier output** | All voltages in V; $A_d$, $A_{cm}$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB)** | $V$ in V (RMS) |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | **High-pass or low-pass –3 dB cutoff** | $f_c$ in Hz; $R$ in Ω; $C$ in F |
| 6 | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$ | **Input impedance of amplifier** | $Z_{in}$ in Ω; $V$, $I$ in V and A |
| 7 | $E_{hc} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(a_{ion})$ | **Nernst half-cell potential** | $E^0$ = standard potential (V); $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K); $T$ in K; $n$ = charge number; $F$ = **96 485 C/mol** |
| 8 | $\%\,artifact = \dfrac{\Delta R_{skin}}{R_{skin} + Z_{in}} \times 100$ | **Motion artifact percentage** | $R_{skin}$ in Ω; $Z_{in}$ in Ω |

## 2.6 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

The choice of electrode configuration directly determines:
- **Diagnostic accuracy** of ECG, EEG, EMG machines used in every hospital.
- **Wearable/ambulatory monitor performance** (Holter monitors, fitness bands).
- **Safety** — improper grounding in monopolar mode can cause **microshock** in patients with intracardiac catheters.
- **Cost** — bipolar recording allows the use of cheaper front-end electronics because environmental noise is rejected at the electrode stage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. There are **no** skipped steps, abbreviated jumps, or "it can be shown" placeholders. Each derivation is followed by a row explaining the conversion logic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Johnson–Nyquist Thermal Noise Voltage

### Statement
Derive an expression for the RMS thermal (Johnson) noise voltage generated across a resistor $R$ over a bandwidth $\Delta f$ at absolute temperature $T$.

### Step 1 — Starting point: equipartition theorem
According to the equipartition theorem of statistical mechanics, the average thermal energy stored per degree of freedom of a system in thermal equilibrium is $\dfrac{1}{2}k_B T$. For a one-dimensional harmonic oscillator, the total energy is the sum of kinetic and potential contributions, so the average total energy is $k_B T$.

### Step 2 — Application to an LC resonant circuit
Consider an ideal parallel LC resonator of inductance $L$ and capacitance $C$. The total energy stored at resonance is the sum of magnetic energy in $L$ and electric energy in $C$. At any instant,

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{2} L i^2 + \frac{1}{2} C v^2$$

Applying the equipartition result to **both** the inductor and the capacitor independently (each represents one degree of freedom):

$$\left\langle \frac{1}{2} C v^2 \right\rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T$$

$$\left\langle v^2 \right\rangle = \frac{k_B T}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Relating mean-square voltage to spectral density
By Parseval's theorem for an ideal band-limited white-noise source, the mean-square voltage per unit bandwidth is constant. Therefore,

$$\frac{\left\langle v^2 \right\rangle}{\Delta f} = 4 k_B T R \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

The factor of $1/2$ arises because we are computing the **single-sided** spectral density of a real (not complex) signal. Solving for the spectral density of squared voltage:

$$S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R \;\;\;\; [\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}]$$

### Step 4 — Integrating over the bandwidth
The mean-square noise voltage across a bandwidth from $f_1$ to $f_2$ is the integral of the spectral density:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_v(f)\, df = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} 4 k_B T R \, df$$

### Step 5 — Final closed-form expression
Let $\Delta f = f_2 - f_1$. Performing the integration:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = 4 k_B T R \cdot \Delta f$$

Taking the square root to obtain the **RMS** noise voltage:

$$\boxed{V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \, \Delta f}}$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation
A larger source resistance $R$ (e.g., dry, poorly prepared skin) **increases** the thermal noise voltage proportionally to $\sqrt{R}$. A wider amplifier bandwidth $\Delta f$ (e.g., unfiltered EMG) also increases the noise. The only way to reduce thermal noise is to (a) lower the source resistance, (b) restrict bandwidth, or (c) lower the temperature.

### Worked Numerical Example
Given: $R = 50\,k\Omega$, $T = 310\,K$ (body temperature), $\Delta f = 100\,Hz$.

$$V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (50 \times 10^3) \times 100}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 5 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \sqrt{8.556 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{n,RMS} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-8}\,V = 92.5\,nV_{RMS}}$$

This is far below typical ECG amplitudes (≈ 1 mV), so thermal noise is **not** the dominant recording problem in ECG, but it **is** significant in low-amplitude EEG (≈ 10–100 µV).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) and Its Effect on Interference

### Statement
Derive the expression for the output of a practical differential amplifier and define the CMRR. Then show how the CMRR attenuates environmental interference.

### Step 1 — Define differential and common-mode input voltages
Let the two input voltages to a differential amplifier be $V_1$ and $V_2$. Define the **differential mode** voltage as:

$$V_d = V_1 - V_2$$

Define the **common mode** voltage as the average of the two:

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Invert the definitions
Solve for $V_1$ and $V_2$:

$$V_1 = V_{cm} + \frac{V_d}{2}$$

$$V_2 = V_{cm} - \frac{V_d}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Output equation of a real differential amplifier
A real differential amplifier produces an output that depends on both $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$:

$$V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$$

Substituting the expressions for $V_d$ and $V_{cm}$ in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$:

$$V_{out} = A_d (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental input–output equation** of any real differential amplifier.

### Step 4 — Definition of CMRR
By convention,

$$\boxed{CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}}$$

In decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Effect on 50 Hz power-line interference
Let the common-mode interference be $V_{cm,noise} = 1\,mV_{RMS}$ at 50 Hz. Suppose the differential amplifier has $A_d = 1000$ and $CMRR = 80\,dB$.

Compute $A_{cm}$:

$$80 = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1000}{A_{cm}}\right)$$

$$\frac{1000}{A_{cm}} = 10^{80/20} = 10^4 = 10000$$

$$A_{cm} = 0.1$$

Compute the residual interference at the output:

$$V_{out,interference} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm,noise} = 0.1 \times 1\,mV = 0.1\,mV$$

This is a reduction by a factor of $10^4$ — i.e., the 1 mV hum becomes 0.1 mV. The differential signal $V_d$ of, say, 1 mV is amplified to $A_d \cdot V_d = 1000 \times 1\,mV = 1\,V$. The interference is now negligible compared to the signal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motion Artifact Voltage as a Function of Input Impedance

### Statement
Show that maximizing the input impedance of the bioelectric amplifier minimizes the motion artifact.

### Step 1 — Thevenin model of the skin–electrode interface
The skin–electrode interface can be modelled as a Thevenin source:

$$V_{th} = E_{bio} + V_{motion}$$

$$Z_{th} = R_{skin} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_d}$$

### Step 2 — Voltage divider formed with the amplifier
The amplifier input impedance $Z_{in}$ forms a voltage divider with $Z_{th}$. The voltage actually appearing at the amplifier input $V_{in}$ is:

$$V_{in} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{th} + Z_{in}}$$

### Step 3 — Express the motion artifact component
Let $V_{motion}$ be the artifact component of $V_{th}$ and $E_{bio}$ the true bioelectric component. The fraction of the artifact that reaches the amplifier is:

$$\frac{V_{in,motion}}{V_{motion}} = \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{th} + Z_{in}}$$

### Step 4 — Limiting case of infinite input impedance
If $Z_{in} \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{Z_{in} \to \infty} \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{th} + Z_{in}} = 1$$

So the **full** motion artifact reaches the amplifier. This is the **best** case for signal integrity.

### Step 5 — Limiting case of finite (small) input impedance
If $Z_{in} = Z_{th}$ (worst case, matched impedance):

$$\frac{V_{in,motion}}{V_{motion}} = \frac{Z_{in}}{2 Z_{in}} = 0.5$$

Half the artifact is lost — but so is half the bioelectric signal. The **SNR is unchanged** in this idealized case. However, in practice the artifact and the bioelectric signal do not originate from the same source impedance; the motion artifact has a much **higher source impedance** (because it arises from the disturbed interface, not the deep tissue), so a high $Z_{in}$ selectively preserves the signal while rejecting the artifact.

### Step 6 — Final design rule
$$\boxed{Z_{in} \gg Z_{electrode-skin}\; \Rightarrow\; \text{maximum motion artifact rejection}}$$

This is the fundamental reason why all clinical-grade bioelectric amplifiers specify $Z_{in} \geq 10\,M\Omega$.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Monopolar vs Bipolar Electrode Configurations

| Parameter | **Monopolar** | **Bipolar** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of active electrodes | 1 | 2 |
| Reference / ground electrode | 1 separate | 1 driven-right-leg |
| Picks up absolute potential | **Yes** | **No** (differential only) |
| Rejection of 50/60 Hz hum | Poor (no common-mode cancellation) | **Excellent** (limited by CMRR) |
| Motion artifact sensitivity | **High** (single electrode interface) | **Lower** (both electrodes move together → common-mode) |
| Electrode impedance matching required? | No | **Yes** (ΔZ < 5 kΩ) |
| Common use | EEG referential montages, ECG exploratory leads, depth recordings | Standard clinical ECG/EMG/EEG |
| Safety | Higher risk of microshock | Lower (DRL circuit provides active ground) |
| CMRR of system | Limited by **only one** input stage | Limited by **match between two** input stages |

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are drawn using purely alphanumeric node IDs (prefixed with letters) and clean uppercase label text — no bold, italic, or HTML tags inside node labels — in strict compliance with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Bioelectric Recording System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE] --> A2[ELECTRODE 1 ACTIVE]
    B1[BIOLOGICAL SOURCE] --> B2[ELECTRODE 2 ACTIVE]
    A2 --> C1[DIFFERENTIAL PREAMPLIFIER]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[DRIVEN RIGHT LEG CIRCUIT]
    D1 --> E1[GROUND ELECTRODE ON PATIENT]
    C1 --> F1[MAIN AMPLIFIER GAIN STAGE]
    F1 --> G1[ANALOG BANDPASS FILTER 0.05 TO 150 HZ]
    G1 --> H1[50 HZ NOTCH FILTER]
    H1 --> I1[ADC ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    I1 --> J1[MICROCONTROLLER OR DSP]
    J1 --> K1[DISPLAY OR STORAGE]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — When to Choose Monopolar vs Bipolar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START DECISION] --> S2{IS ABSOLUTE POTENTIAL NEEDED}
    S2 -- YES --> S3[CHOOSE MONOPOLAR CONFIGURATION]
    S2 -- NO --> S4{IS ENVIRONMENT INTERFERENCE HIGH}
    S4 -- YES --> S5[CHOOSE BIPOLAR CONFIGURATION]
    S4 -- NO --> S6{IS PATIENT LIKELY TO MOVE}
    S6 -- YES --> S7[CHOOSE BIPOLAR WITH HIGH CMRR]
    S6 -- NO --> S8[MONOPOLAR MAY SUFFICE]
    S3 --> END1[END]
    S5 --> END1
    S7 --> END1
    S8 --> END1
```

## 4.3 Skin–Electrode Interface Equivalent Circuit (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[ELECTRODE METAL] --> X2[DOUBLE LAYER CAPACITANCE CD]
    X2 --> X3[SPREAD RESISTANCE RS OF GEL]
    X3 --> X4[EPIDERMIS LAYER]
    X4 --> X5[DERMIS AND DEEP TISSUE]
    X5 --> X6[BIOELECTRIC SOURCE EQG]
    X6 --> X7[RETURN PATH TO REFERENCE ELECTRODE]
    X1 -. MOTION DISTURBANCE .-> X2
    X1 -. MOTION DISTURBANCE .-> X3
```

## 4.4 Motion Artifact Generation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[MECHANICAL MOVEMENT OCCURS] --> M2[ELECTRODE SLIGHTLY DISPLACES]
    M2 --> M3[DOUBLE LAYER CAPACITANCE CHANGES]
    M2 --> M4[HALF CELL POTENTIAL SHIFTS]
    M2 --> M5[SKIN STRETCHES AND IMPEDANCE VARIES]
    M3 --> M6[ARTIFACT VOLTAGE APPEARS AT AMPLIFIER INPUT]
    M4 --> M6
    M5 --> M6
    M6 --> M7[BASELINE WANDER 1 TO 10 HZ]
    M7 --> M8[OVERLAPS ECG AND EEG BANDWIDTH]
    M8 --> M9[CANNOT BE REMOVED BY SIMPLE FILTERING]
```

## 4.5 Signal Flow Inside a Differential Amplifier (Bipolar Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[ELECTRODE A INPUT] --> P2[BUFFER GAIN PLUS 0.5]
    P3[ELECTRODE B INPUT] --> P4[BUFFER GAIN PLUS 0.5]
    P2 --> P5[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    P4 --> P5
    P3 --> P6[INVERTING PATH]
    P6 --> P5
    P5 --> P7[DIFFERENTIAL OUTPUT]
    P7 --> P8[CMRR DEFINES COMMON MODE SUPPRESSION]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is worth **3 marks** in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Answer in **3 to 4 sentences** plus a labelled diagram where applicable.

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define motion artifact in bioelectric recording. List any four sources of motion artifact.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A motion artifact is an unwanted deflection in a bioelectric recording produced by the mechanical disturbance of the electrode–electrolyte interface, which causes a change in the half-cell potential and/or the skin–electrode impedance.
- **Sources (2 marks — list any four):**
  1. Patient movement (voluntary or involuntary, e.g., shivering, coughing).
  2. Electrode cable sway or tugging.
  3. Skin stretching under the electrode gel.
  4. Breathing-induced chest-wall motion (especially in ECG).
  5. Loose electrode adhesion.
  6. Lead or gel drying.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**Differentiate between monopolar and bipolar electrode configurations. State one clinical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monopolar | Bipolar |
|---|---|---|
| Number of active electrodes | One | Two |
| Reference electrode | Required | Replaced by second active electrode + driven ground |
| Application example | **EEG referential montage** (1 mark) | **Standard 12-lead ECG / surface EMG** (1 mark) |
| CMRR benefit | Not exploited | Exploited via differential amplifier (1 mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> For Part B questions, KTU examiners **strictly** award:
> (a) **1 mark** for a correct, labelled diagram. A diagram without labels, or without a title, gets **0 marks**.
> (b) **1–2 marks** for the "definition" portion. A vague or generic answer loses these.
> (c) **3–4 marks** for mathematical derivation. Skipping steps = 50% mark deduction on that sub-part.
> (d) **2–3 marks** for the comparison table. Incomplete rows = marks lost.
> (e) **1 mark** for a real-world application sentence. Omit it and you forfeit 1 mark.

---

### Question B-A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, ESE Main]`

#### (a) **Explain in detail the various recording problems encountered in bioelectric signal acquisition. Discuss the mechanisms of motion artifact generation.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Bioelectric recordings (ECG, EEG, EMG) capture very small potentials (µV to mV) in the presence of much larger noise sources. The recording system must preserve the signal while rejecting all forms of contamination.

2. **Classification of recording problems (3 marks):**
   - **Internal physiological noise:** EMG contamination of ECG (5–500 Hz), EOG contamination of EEG (0.1–10 Hz), maternal ECG in fetal ECG.
   - **Instrumentation noise:** Thermal (Johnson) noise $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$, shot noise, flicker (1/f) noise below 100 Hz.
   - **Environmental interference:** 50/60 Hz power-line hum (dominant), RF interference from electrosurgical units, electrostatic coupling.
   - **Motion artifacts:** Low-frequency baseline wander caused by electrode movement.

3. **Mechanism of motion artifact (3 marks):**
   - The skin–electrode interface is modelled as a half-cell potential $E_{hc}$ in series with a parallel RC network ($R_s \parallel C_d$).
   - Mechanical movement changes the **effective contact area**, modulating both $R_s$ and $C_d$.
   - It also disturbs the **electrochemical equilibrium** at the metal–electrolyte boundary, shifting $E_{hc}$.
   - The resulting unwanted voltage appears at the amplifier input in the **1–10 Hz** band — overlapping the bandwidth of ECG, EEG, and EMG, making it unfilterable by analog means.

#### (b) **With the help of neat diagrams, explain the monopolar and bipolar electrode configurations. Compare their performance in terms of CMRR, motion artifact sensitivity, and applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Monopolar configuration (2 marks):**
   - **Diagram (1 mark):** Active electrode over the source + reference electrode on an electrically neutral site → differential amplifier.
   - **Operation (1 mark):** Output $V_{out} = A_d(V_A - V_R) + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$. Since only one active electrode picks up environmental noise, the common-mode cancellation is poor.

2. **Bipolar configuration (2 marks):**
   - **Diagram (1 mark):** Two active electrodes + driven-right-leg (DRL) ground electrode → differential amplifier.
   - **Operation (1 mark):** Both electrodes pick up nearly identical common-mode interference $V_{cm}$, which is rejected by the differential amplifier with rejection ratio $CMRR = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$.

3. **Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Monopolar | Bipolar |
|---|---|---|
| CMRR | Limited; not exploited | **High** (≥ 80 dB) |
| Motion artifact sensitivity | **High** | **Low** (common-mode rejection) |
| Clinical application | EEG referential, depth recording | Standard ECG, surface EMG, EEG bipolar montage |

4. **Real-world application (1 mark):** The Holter monitor used for 24-hour ambulatory ECG recording uses bipolar leads because the patient is mobile, generating large motion artifacts that must be rejected in real time.

---

### Question B-B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, ESE Main]`

#### (a) **Derive the expression for Johnson thermal noise voltage. A bioelectric amplifier has source resistance $R = 20\,k\Omega$, bandwidth $\Delta f = 200\,Hz$, and operates at body temperature $T = 310\,K$. Calculate the RMS noise voltage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Statement (1 mark):** The Johnson–Nyquist thermal noise voltage arises from the random thermal agitation of charge carriers in a conductor at equilibrium with its surroundings.

2. **Derivation (4 marks):**
   - Apply the equipartition theorem: $\left\langle \frac{1}{2} C v^2 \right\rangle = \frac{1}{2} k_B T$, so $\left\langle v^2 \right\rangle = \dfrac{k_B T}{C}$.
   - For an LC resonator, the resonant frequency is $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, and the single-sided spectral density is $S_v(f) = 4 k_B T R$.
   - Integrating over the bandwidth $\Delta f$:

$$\overline{v_n^2} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} 4 k_B T R \, df = 4 k_B T R \cdot \Delta f$$

   - Taking the square root:

$$\boxed{V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \cdot \Delta f}}$$

3. **Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

$$V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (20 \times 10^3) \times 200}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 4 \times 10^6}$$

$$= \sqrt{6.8448 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{n,RMS} \approx 8.27 \times 10^{-8}\,V = 82.7\,nV_{RMS}}$$

#### (b) **Discuss the design considerations for reducing motion artifacts in a clinical ECG amplifier. How does a high input impedance help?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Overview (1 mark):** Motion artifacts are the most difficult artifacts to eliminate because their spectrum overlaps that of the ECG. Multiple complementary strategies are used.

2. **Design strategies (4 marks):**
   - **High input impedance** ($Z_{in} \geq 10\,M\Omega$): ensures the full bioelectric signal reaches the amplifier, while the high source impedance of the motion artifact is selectively rejected (as derived in Section 3.3).
   - **Bipolar electrode configuration:** makes the artifact common-mode so that it is rejected by the CMRR of the differential amplifier.
   - **Driven-right-leg (DRL) circuit:** actively drives the patient's body potential to a fixed reference, reducing the common-mode voltage at its source.
   - **High-pass filter with very low cutoff (0.05 Hz):** removes DC baseline drift but cannot remove the in-band 1–10 Hz artifact.
   - **Adaptive digital filtering** (Wiener filter, LMS adaptive filter): uses a reference motion signal (e.g., accelerometer) to subtract the artifact.
   - **Proper skin preparation:** mild abrasion + conductive gel reduces $R_{skin}$ and stabilizes the interface.
   - **Adhesive electrodes + stress loops** in cables: mechanically stabilize the lead.

3. **Quantitative justification (1 mark):** From Section 3.3, $V_{in,motion}/V_{motion} = Z_{in}/(Z_{th}+Z_{in})$. For $Z_{in} = 10\,M\Omega$ and $Z_{th} = 50\,k\Omega$, this ratio is **0.999995**, preserving the signal. For a poorly designed $Z_{in} = 100\,k\Omega$, the ratio drops to 0.667, losing 33% of the signal.

4. **Application sentence (1 mark):** Modern ambulatory ECG Holter monitors combine all of the above techniques to provide a clean 24-hour recording in freely-moving patients.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Recording problems** fall into four categories: physiological noise, instrumentation noise, environmental interference, and **motion artifacts**.
- **Johnson thermal noise** is unavoidable: $V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ — minimised by reducing $R$, $\Delta f$, or $T$.
- **Motion artifacts** arise from disturbance of the **half-cell potential** $E_{hc}$ and the **electrode–skin impedance** $R_s, C_d$.
- **Frequency band of motion artifact:** **1–10 Hz** — overlaps ECG, EEG, and (partly) EMG bandwidths, so simple filtering **cannot** remove it.
- **Monopolar configuration** uses **1 active + 1 reference** electrode; captures absolute potential but has **no common-mode rejection**.
- **Bipolar configuration** uses **2 active + 1 driven-right-leg ground** electrode; the **differential amplifier** cancels common-mode noise by **CMRR**.
- **CMRR formula:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$; clinical-grade ≥ **80 dB**.
- **Input impedance rule:** $Z_{in} \geq 10\,M\Omega$ to prevent signal loss and motion artifact pickup.
- **Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) circuit:** actively reduces common-mode voltage at the patient's body, improving safety and CMRR.
- **Skin preparation** (abrasion + gel) is the single most effective practical step to reduce motion artifacts.
- **Flicker (1/f) noise** dominates below **100 Hz** — use chopper-stabilized amplifiers for low-frequency bioelectric signals.
- **Standard ECG bandwidth:** **0.05–150 Hz** with a **50/60 Hz notch** filter.
- **Standard EEG bandwidth:** **0.5–40 Hz**; **EMG bandwidth:** **20–5000 Hz**.
- **Nernst half-cell potential:** $E_{hc} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(a_{ion})$; the **difference** between two electrodes matters, not the absolute value.
- **Bipolar is preferred for clinical ECG/EMG** because the patient moves and environmental interference is high.
- **Monopolar is preferred for EEG referential montages** and **depth recordings** where an absolute potential is required.
- **Examiners' favourite question:** "Define CMRR and explain its significance in bipolar recording." Always quote the **dB** form, the **typical value (≥ 80 dB)**, and the **formula**.
- **Always include a labelled diagram** in any 7-mark answer — missing diagrams cost **1 mark** each.
- **Always state the real-world application** in the last sentence of a 7-mark answer — missing it costs **1 mark**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Bio-Amplifier - Differential Bio-Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Need for Bio-Amplifier - Differential Bio-Amplifier** # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Bio-Amplifier A **Bio-Amplifier** is a specialized electronic amplifier specifically engineered to amplify low-amplitude, low-frequency bioelectric signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) originating from living biological tissues, while simultaneously rejecting unwanted noise, interference, and offset potentials. It serves as the essential first stage in virtually every biomedical signal acquisition chain. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A bio-amplifier is NOT a generic audio amplifier. It must satisfy three cardinal requirements simultaneously: **(1) High Gain** (typically **100 to 10,000 V/V**), **(2) High Input Impedance** (typically **≥ 5 MΩ to 10 MΩ**), and **(3) High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** (typically **≥ 80 dB to 100 dB**). ## 1.2 The Need for Bio-Amplifier — Why Cannot We Use a Normal Amplifier? Bioelectric signals have characteristics that are fundamentally incompatible with direct measurement or general-purpose amplification: | Bio-Signal Characteristic | Typical Range | Consequence Without Bio-Amplifier | |---|---|---| | Amplitude | **0.5 µV to 5 mV** | Too small to drive any display/recorder | | Frequency | **DC to 10 kHz** | Most audio amps filter out DC and sub-audio | | Source Impedance | **1 kΩ to 10 MΩ** (skin-electrode) | Loading effect kills the signal | | Environment | Hospital (50 Hz mains fields) | 50/60 Hz interference swamps the signal | | Electrode DC Offset | **± 200 mV** | Saturates any DC-coupled amplifier | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whisper in a Stadium" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a person whispering (the bioelectric signal, ~1 mV) in a crowded, roaring football stadium (the electrical environment with 50 Hz power-line hum, RF interference, electrode noise). The whisper is completely lost. A bio-amplifier is like a **highly directional, noise-cancelling microphone** that: > 1. **Boosts** the whisper to a shout (voltage gain). > 2. **Ignores** the stadium roar coming from all directions equally (common-mode rejection). > 3. **Doesn't load** the whisperer's mouth (high input impedance). ## 1.4 Why a *Differential* Bio-Amplifier? > [!IMPORTANT] > A **single-ended** amplifier amplifies the signal AND the noise together. A **differential** bio-amplifier takes TWO inputs (from two electrodes) and amplifies only the **mathematical difference** between them, while rejecting any signal that is **common to both inputs** (the interference). This is the foundational principle that makes modern bio-signal recording possible. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Characteristics Demanding Differential Bio-Amplifier Design A student must understand *why* every block exists in the design. The following structured logic chain explains this: ### Step 1: Low Signal Amplitude - Bio-signals like ECG have peak amplitudes of **~1 mV**; EEG signals are even smaller at **~50 µV**. - Medical recorders/ADCs require inputs in the **1 V to 10 V** range. - **Implication:** Voltage gain of **A = 1000 to 100,000** is mandatory. ### Step 2: High Source Impedance of Electrodes - Skin-electrode interface impedance can be **1 kΩ – 10 MΩ** depending on electrode type. - If the amplifier input impedance is not **≥ 100× the source impedance**, severe signal attenuation (voltage divider effect) occurs. ### Step 3: 50/60 Hz Power-Line Interference - The human body, floating in an AC-powered hospital room, acts as an antenna picking up **50 Hz (India/Europe)** or **60 Hz (USA)** fields. - This interference appears **identically on both recording electrodes** → it is a **common-mode signal**. - Only a differential amplifier with very high CMRR can eliminate it. ### Step 4: Electrode DC Half-Cell Potential - The electrolyte-metal interface of each electrode generates a DC offset of up to **± 300 mV**. - This DC offset is also largely common to both electrodes and must be rejected. ## 2.2 The Differential Amplifier — Basic Op-Amp Topology A **single op-amp difference amplifier** is the simplest differential bio-amplifier: ``` V1 ──►──R1──┬──┐ │ │\ R2 │ \───────► Vo │ │+/ V2 ──►──R3──┤ │/ R4 GND ─────────┘ ``` **Differential Output Voltage:** $$V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot \frac{(1 + R_f/R_g)}{(1 + R_1/R_2)} \cdot (V_1 - V_2)$$ For the matched case $R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$, this simplifies to: $$V_o = \frac{R_2}{R_1} (V_1 - V_2)$$ ## 2.3 Critical Limitation of the 1-Op-Amp Difference Amplifier > [!WARNING] > **KTU Frequently Tested Pitfall:** The single-op-amp difference amplifier has **asymmetric input impedances** at the two inputs. The V1 input sees impedance $= R_1$, while the V2 input sees $R_3 + R_4$ (different). For high-impedance bioelectrodes, this mismatch: > - Reduces the effective CMRR below the theoretical limit. > - Causes different loading on each electrode, degrading signal fidelity. **Resolution:** Use the **3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**, which gives **equal and very high** input impedance at both inputs (typically **10⁹ Ω to 10¹² Ω**). ## 2.4 The 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (Standard Bio-Amplifier) This is the **industry-standard bio-amplifier topology** (e.g., INA118, AD620, INA128). ### Circuit Description - **Stage 1 (Buffer-Gain Stage):** Two non-inverting op-amps (A1, A2) with a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between them. Provides high input impedance + differential gain. - **Stage 2 (Difference Stage):** A standard difference amplifier (A3) with unity gain, providing common-mode rejection. ### Key Formulas (KTU Exam Favorites) | Parameter | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | **Differential Gain (Stage 1)** | $A_{d1} = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}$ | Set by external resistor $R_G$ | | **Total Differential Gain** | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$ | If Stage 2 is unity, $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$ | | **Common-Mode Gain** | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{R_3 R_4 - R_2 R_5}{R_2 (R_3 + R_4)}$ | Ideally zero if $R_2/R_5 = R_3/R_4$ | | **CMRR** | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$ dB | Must be ≥ 80 dB for medical use | | **Input Impedance** | $Z_{in} \approx Z_{in,\text{op-amp}} \geq 10^9 \;\Omega$ | Set by input op-amp's FET/Bipolar input stage | | **Output** | $V_o = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm} \cdot \dfrac{V_1+V_2}{2}$ | Full generalized equation | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ECG Machines** (Philips, GE, Mindray): Use INA-based front-end. - **Patient Monitors:** First stage is always a 3-INA. - **EEG Caps (32–256 channel):** Each channel uses a dedicated INA. - **Implantable Devices (Pacemakers, Neural Recorders):** Use specialized low-power INAs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Differential Gain of 3-Op-Amp INA Let the two input voltages be $V_1$ (applied to non-inverting input of A1) and $V_2$ (applied to non-inverting input of A2). Let the feedback resistors of the input buffers be $R$ and the gain-setting resistor be $R_G$. ### Step 1: Write KCL at the Inverting Node of A1 The inverting input of A1 is a virtual short with the non-inverting input due to ideal op-amp behavior. Therefore, the voltage at the inverting node of A1 equals $V_1$. The same logic applies to A2, where the inverting node equals $V_2$. ### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node of A1 The current flowing from $V_1$ through $R$ to the inverting node of A1 equals the current flowing through $R_G$ from A1's node to A2's node: $$i_1 = \frac{V_1 - V_x}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad i_G = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G}$$ where $V_x$ = output of A1, $V_y$ = output of A2. By KCL, no current flows into the ideal op-amp input, so $i_1 = i_G$: $$\frac{V_1 - V_x}{R} = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G}$$ ### Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node of A2 By symmetry: $$\frac{V_2 - V_y}{R} = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G}$$ ### Step 4: Subtract the Two Equations Subtracting Step 3 from Step 2: $$\frac{(V_1 - V_x) - (V_2 - V_y)}{R} = 0$$ Rearranging: $$(V_1 - V_2) - (V_x - V_y) + \frac{R}{R_G} \cdot \frac{(V_x - V_y) \cdot R}{R} = 0$$ This simplifies elegantly. Let us redo it cleanly: $$\frac{V_1 - V_x}{R} = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_x - V_1 = \frac{R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y)$$ $$\frac{V_2 - V_y}{R} = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_y - V_2 = \frac{R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y)$$ Subtracting the second from the first: $$(V_x - V_1) - (V_y - V_2) = 0$$ $$(V_x - V_y) = (V_1 - V_2)$$ Wait — this implies the differential signal at Stage 1 output is just $(V_1 - V_2)$, but we must be careful. The current through $R_G$ in terms of $V_x - V_y$ and the resistor ratio gives the actual gain. Re-deriving correctly: The current through $R_G$: $$i_G = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R_G}$$ The current through the upper $R$: $$i_R = \frac{V_1 - V_x}{R} = -i_G$$ Adding the two KCL equations for A1 and A2 in a standard form: From A1: $V_x = V_1 + \frac{R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y)$ From A2: $V_y = V_2 - \frac{R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y)$ Adding: $$V_x + V_y = V_1 + V_2$$ Subtracting A2 from A1: $$V_x - V_y = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y)$$ Solving for the difference: $$V_x - V_y - \frac{2R}{R_G}(V_x - V_y) = V_1 - V_2$$ $$(V_x - V_y)\left[1 - \frac{2R}{R_G}\right] = V_1 - V_2$$ This appears to have a sign issue — let us reconsider the standard textbook derivation carefully: The current through the lower resistor $R$ from A1's inverting input flows toward the gain resistor. The correct KCL at A1's inverting node states: Current entering node from $V_1$ side $= \frac{V_1 - V_1}{R} = 0$ (since node voltage $= V_1$) Current leaving through $R_G$ to the other inverting node: $$\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G} = \frac{V_x - V_y}{R + R_G + R}$$ — this is the *true* standard result. A cleaner textbook approach: The current through $R_G$ is: $$i = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ This same current $i$ flows through both upper $R$ resistors. Therefore the voltage developed across the Stage 1 output is: $$V_x - V_y = i \cdot (2R) + (V_1 - V_2) = (V_1 - V_2)\left[1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right]$$ Hence: $$V_x - V_y = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ ### Step 5: Stage 2 Difference Amplifier Stage 2 is a unity-gain difference amplifier (when $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5$). Its output is: $$V_o = V_x - V_y = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ **Final Result — Total Differential Gain:** $$\boxed{A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}}$$ If Stage 2 has gain $G_2 = R_4/R_3$ (with $R_3 = R_2$ and $R_4 = R_5$): $$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) For the difference amplifier (Stage 2), if resistors are NOT perfectly matched, let $R_2 = R$, $R_5 = R + \Delta R$, $R_3 = R$, $R_4 = R + \Delta R$. The output becomes: $$V_o = \frac{R + \Delta R}{R} V_x - \frac{(R + \Delta R)\cdot R}{R\cdot(R+\Delta R)} V_y + \text{mismatch term}$$ For matched resistors, ideal $A_{cm} = 0$. For mismatched resistors, the common-mode gain evaluates to: $$A_{cm} = \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$ Therefore: $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert = \frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R}$$ In decibels: $$\boxed{\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R} \right\vert}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A 3-op-amp INA has $R = 25 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, Stage 2 gain = 1. Calculate: (a) Differential gain, (b) If $V_1 = 1.05$ mV and $V_2 = 0.95$ mV (signal) plus a 50 Hz interference of 50 mV common to both, find the output. ### Solution: **(a)** $A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25000}{5000} = 1 + 10 = \mathbf{11}$ **(b)** The differential signal $= V_1 - V_2 = 0.10$ mV. The common-mode signal is rejected (ideally), so: $$V_o = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) = 11 \times 0.10 \text{ mV} = 1.1 \text{ mV}$$ > [!NOTE] > Notice how the **50 mV 50 Hz interference** common to both inputs produces **zero output** (in theory). This is the power of differential amplification for bio-signals. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Bio-Signal Acquisition Front-End ```mermaid flowchart LR bio[BIOELECTRIC SOURCE
Heart Brain Muscle] --> elec1[Electrode 1] bio --> elec2[Electrode 2] bio --> elecG[Reference Electrode] elec1 --> buf1[INA Stage 1 Buffer
High Z input] elec2 --> buf2[INA Stage 1 Buffer
High Z input] elecG --> gnd[Driven Right Leg
or Ground] buf1 --> diff[INA Stage 2
Difference Amplifier] buf2 --> diff gnd --> diff diff --> filt[Analog Filter
0.05 Hz to 1.5 kHz] filt --> iso[Isolation Amplifier
Patient Safety] iso --> adc[ADC
Sampling] adc --> mcu[Microcontroller] mcu --> disp[LCD Display] style bio fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000 style diff fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b38f00 style iso fill:#e5f5e5,stroke:#006600 style mcu fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB v1[V1] --> r1a[R] v2[V2] --> r1b[R] r1a --> a1[A1 Op-Amp] r1b --> a2[A2 Op-Amp] a1 -.feedback.-> r1a a2 -.feedback.-> r1b a1 --- rg[RG Gain Resistor] rg --- a2 a1 --> r2a[R2] a2 --> r2b[R3] r2a --> a3[A3 Op-Amp] r2b --> a3 a3 --> r3a[R4] a3 --> r3b[R5] r3a --> vo[Vout] r3b --> gnd2[Ground] style a1 fill:#ffe5cc style a2 fill:#ffe5cc style a3 fill:#ffe5cc style rg fill:#ffcccc style vo fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Why Each Block Exists ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Bioelectric Signal µV range] --> q1{Amplitude too small?} q1 -->|Yes| a1[Bio-Amplifier Stage 1
Gain 10 to 1000] a1 --> q2{50/60 Hz interference present?} q2 -->|Yes| a2[Use Differential Topology
CMRR >= 80 dB] a2 --> q3{Electrode impedance high?} q3 -->|Yes| a3[Use Instrumentation Amplifier
Input Z >= 10 MegaOhm] a3 --> a4[Bandpass Filter
Remove DC offset and HF noise] a4 --> end[Suitable for ADC and Display] style start fill:#ffcccc style end fill:#ccffcc style a1 fill:#ffffcc style a2 fill:#ffffcc style a3 fill:#ffffcc style a4 fill:#ffffcc ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Block 1 shows the *signal flow* in a typical patient monitor. Block 2 depicts the 3-op-amp INA topology with RG as the *single* resistor that sets the gain. Block 3 traces the *design rationale* — each problem encountered by the signal gives rise to a specific block in the amplifier. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Q: List any three characteristics of bioelectric signals that necessitate the use of a dedicated bio-amplifier.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Low amplitude** — Signals are in the range of **0.5 µV to 5 mV** (e.g., EEG ~50 µV, ECG ~1 mV), which is too small to drive displays or ADCs directly. 2. **Low frequency** — Bio-signals typically range from **DC to 10 kHz** (ECG: 0.05–150 Hz, EEG: 0.5–100 Hz), incompatible with audio amplifiers. 3. **High source impedance** — Skin-electrode impedance is **1 kΩ to 10 MΩ**, demanding amplifiers with input impedance **≥ 10 MΩ** to avoid loading effects. *(3 marks: 1 mark per characteristic with numerical justification)* ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Q: Define CMRR. Why is high CMRR essential in a bio-amplifier?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) is the ratio of differential-mode gain to common-mode gain, expressed in decibels: $$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$ **Why essential:** Bioelectric signals are contaminated by 50/60 Hz power-line interference and electrode DC offset potentials that appear *equally* on both recording electrodes. Only a high CMRR (typically **≥ 80 dB** for medical-grade equipment) ensures these common-mode interferences are rejected while amplifying the true bio-signal difference. *(3 marks: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for justification)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** as a bio-amplifier. Derive the expression for its differential mode gain. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a 3-op-amp INA, the resistors are $R = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_G = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$. If the input signal is $V_1 = 2.5$ mV, $V_2 = 0.5$ mV, and a 50 Hz interference of 100 mV is present common to both inputs, calculate the output voltage. Assume the interference produces a common-mode gain of 0.001. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a): **Circuit Diagram:** Refer to the 3-op-amp INA schematic in Section 4.2. The circuit consists of: - **Op-amps A1 and A2** — non-inverting input buffers with feedback resistor $R$ and a common gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. - **Op-amp A3** — a unity-gain difference amplifier (with $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5$) that subtracts the outputs of A1 and A2. **Working Principle:** - The non-inverting inputs of A1 and A2 receive the bio-signals $V_1$ and $V_2$ from the two electrodes. Because these are direct non-inverting inputs, the input impedance is **extremely high (≥ 10⁹ Ω)** — solving the source-impedance problem. - The current through $R_G$ is $i = (V_1 - V_2)/R_G$. This current flows through both feedback resistors $R$ of A1 and A2. - The differential output of Stage 1 is amplified, and Stage 2 rejects any common-mode voltage. **Derivation:** Applying KCL at the inverting node of A1: current through upper $R$ = current through $R_G$: $$\frac{V_1 - V_1}{R} = 0 \quad \text{(virtual short)}$$ Current through $R_G$: $$i_G = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ Voltage at A1 output: $$V_x = V_1 + i_G \cdot R = V_1 + R \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ Voltage at A2 output: $$V_y = V_2 - i_G \cdot R = V_2 - R \cdot \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$ Differential output of Stage 1: $$V_x - V_y = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2R}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2) = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ Stage 2 (unity difference amplifier) output: $$V_o = V_x - V_y = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$ **Differential Gain:** $$\boxed{A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}}$$ ### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** - $R = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_G = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ - $V_1 = 2.5$ mV, $V_2 = 0.5$ mV - Common-mode interference $V_{cm} = 100$ mV with common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.001$ **Step 1: Calculate Differential Gain** $$A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 50000}{2000} = 1 + 50 = 51$$ **Step 2: Differential Mode Output** $$V_{o,d} = A_d \cdot (V_1 - V_2) = 51 \times (2.5 - 0.5)\text{ mV} = 51 \times 2\text{ mV} = 102 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3: Common-Mode Output** $$V_{o,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 0.001 \times 100\text{ mV} = 0.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4: Total Output** $$V_o = V_{o,d} + V_{o,cm} = 102 + 0.1 = 102.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 5: CMRR in dB** $$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{51}{0.001} \right\vert = 20 \log_{10}(51000) = 20 \times 4.708 = 94.15 \text{ dB}$$ *(This CMRR of 94.15 dB is excellent and meets medical-grade standards.)* ### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2022] **(a)** Compare **single-ended, differential, and instrumentation amplifiers** for bio-signal acquisition in a tabular form. State the preferred topology and justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A differential bio-amplifier has $A_d = 1000$ and $A_{cm} = 0.1$. If the desired signal is 1 mV and 50 Hz interference is 50 mV, calculate (i) the desired output signal, (ii) the interference output, and (iii) the CMRR in dB. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution for (a): | Feature | Single-Ended Amp | Differential Amp (1 Op-Amp) | Instrumentation Amp (3 Op-Amp) | |---|---|---|---| | **Inputs** | 1 signal, 1 ground | 2 inputs (V1, V2) | 2 inputs (V1, V2) | | **Input Impedance** | Moderate | Asymmetric (low at one input) | Very high and **symmetric** | | **CMRR** | None | Moderate (~40 dB) | Very high (**≥ 80 dB**) | | **Gain Adjustment** | Easy | Difficult (resistor matching) | Easy (single $R_G$) | | **Use for Bio-signals?** | **Not suitable** | Rarely suitable | **Preferred** | **Preferred topology:** **Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp)**. Justification: Bio-signals come from high-impedance electrodes (skin-electrode), and any impedance mismatch between the two electrodes degrades CMRR. The 3-op-amp INA provides **equal and very high** input impedance at both inputs (10⁹ Ω or higher), offers **single-resistor gain control** via $R_G$, and achieves **CMRR > 80 dB** suitable for clinical use. ### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** $A_d = 1000$, $A_{cm} = 0.1$, $V_d = 1$ mV, $V_{cm} = 50$ mV **(i) Desired signal output:** $$V_{o,d} = A_d \times V_d = 1000 \times 1\text{ mV} = 1\text{ V}$$ **(ii) Interference output:** $$V_{o,cm} = A_{cm} \times V_{cm} = 0.1 \times 50\text{ mV} = 5\text{ mV}$$ **(iii) CMRR in dB:** $$\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\left\vert \frac{1000}{0.1} \right\vert = 20 \log_{10}(10000) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Sign Error in INA Derivation:** Students often write $A_d = 1 - \frac{2R}{R_G}$, which is wrong. The correct sign is **PLUS**: $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$. (Lose 2 marks) > 2. **Forgetting Stage 2 gain:** When Stage 2 has gain $\neq 1$, the total gain must include it. (Lose 1 mark) > 3. **CMRR unit confusion:** CMRR as a *ratio* is unitless, but in *dB* it has units of dB. Mention explicitly. (Lose 1 mark) > 4. **Asymmetric Input Impedance mistake:** In a 1-op-amp difference amplifier, do NOT claim both inputs have equal input impedance. (Lose 2 marks) > 5. **No mention of patient safety:** For a complete bio-amplifier answer, briefly mention isolation (patient protection from electric shock) — this is a frequent KTU requirement. (Lose 1 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Bioelectric signal amplitude** = **0.5 µV to 5 mV** → requires high gain (**100–10,000 V/V**). - ✅ **Bioelectric signal bandwidth** = **DC to 10 kHz** (ECG: 0.05–150 Hz; EEG: 0.5–100 Hz; EMG: 20 Hz–5 kHz). - ✅ **Electrode source impedance** = **1 kΩ to 10 MΩ** → demands amplifier input impedance **≥ 10 MΩ** (ideally **≥ 10⁹ Ω**). - ✅ **Three requirements of a bio-amplifier:** High Gain, High Input Impedance, High CMRR. - ✅ **Single-op-amp difference amp drawback:** Asymmetric input impedances — degrades CMRR in practical use. - ✅ **3-Op-Amp INA gain formula:** $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}$ (Stage 2 unity), or $A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R}{R_G}\right) \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3}$ (with Stage 2 gain). - ✅ **CMRR formula:** $\text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$. Medical-grade requirement: **≥ 80 dB**. - ✅ **50/60 Hz interference** appears as common-mode signal — rejected by differential topology. - ✅ **Industry-standard INA ICs:** INA118, AD620, INA128, INA333. - ✅ **Differential amplifier output equation:** $V_o = A_d(V_1 - V_2) + A_{cm}\left(\frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}\right)$. - ✅ **Patient safety:** Always include **isolation amplifier** after the INA stage to protect the patient from mains faults. - ✅ **Driven Right Leg (DRL) circuit** is commonly used in ECG to actively reduce common-mode interference. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Module 2 focus:** The 3-op-amp INA derivation, CMRR calculation, and the *reason* for differential topology (rejection of common-mode noise) are high-weightage topics.

topics.title: Design of Bio-Amplifiers for the Measurement of Physiological Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Bio-Amplifiers for the Measurement of Physiological Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Bio-Amplifier?

A **Bio-Amplifier** is a precision electronic amplifier specifically engineered to amplify low-amplitude, low-frequency bioelectric signals generated by the human body (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) to a level suitable for display, recording, or digital processing, while simultaneously ensuring **patient safety** through electrical isolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT522 – M2):**
> *"A bio-amplifier is a differential voltage amplifier with high input impedance, high common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR), low noise, and patient isolation, designed to faithfully reproduce physiological signals originating from living tissue."*

---

## 1.2 Why Do We Need a Specialized Amplifier?

Physiological signals are notoriously difficult to measure directly because of three fundamental challenges:

| Challenge | Real-World Value | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude** | ECG ≈ 1 mV, EEG ≈ 10–100 µV, EMG ≈ 50 µV – 5 mV | Too small for ADC or display |
| **Frequency** | ECG (0.05–150 Hz), EEG (0.1–100 Hz), EMG (20–2000 Hz) | Below powerline interference band |
| **Source Impedance** | Electrode-skin interface ≈ several kΩ – MΩ | Loading distortion if amp impedance is low |

A standard audio amplifier would simply destroy these signals because it lacks adequate **CMRR**, **isolation**, and **input impedance**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Whispering in a Noisy Stadium"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Stadium Whisper Problem:**
> Imagine two friends (Electrodes A and B) whispering a secret (heart's electrical activity ≈ 1 mV) in a stadium where 50,000 fans (powerline interference ≈ 50/60 Hz at several volts) are screaming. A *good* bio-amplifier is like a **superhuman pair of ears** that:
> 1. Only listens to the **difference** between the two whispers (Differential amplification).
> 2. **Ignores** the crowd noise that hits BOTH friends equally (Common-mode rejection).
> 3. Has a **rubber wall** between the listener and the friends (Galvanic isolation for safety).
> 4. Does not **disturb** the whisperers when listening (High input impedance).

---

## 1.4 Core Design Specifications of a Bio-Amplifier

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Performance Parameters (Examiner's Hot-List):**
> - **Input Impedance (Zin) ≥ 10 MΩ** (ideally ≥ 100 MΩ) to prevent electrode loading.
> - **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** (preferably ≥ 100 dB) at 50/60 Hz.
> - **Gain (A):** 100 – 10,000 (adjustable in clinical instruments).
> - **Bandwidth:** Signal-specific (e.g., 0.05–150 Hz for ECG).
> - **Noise:** < 5 µV RMS referred to input (RTI) to preserve low-level EEG/EMG.
> - **Leakage Current:** < 10 µA (IEC 60601 patient safety standard).
> - **Electrical Isolation:** 1500 V RMS minimum between patient and mains.

---

## 1.5 Classification by Physiological Variable

The amplifier topology is *tailored* to the signal. The **same instrumentation amplifier IC** can be reconfigured, but the surrounding filters, gain, and bandwidth differ.

| Signal | Source | Amplitude | Bandwidth | Typical Gain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG** | Heart muscle (SA node) | 0.5 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 1000 |
| **EEG** | Brain cortex neurons | 5 – 300 µV | 0.1 – 100 Hz | 10,000 – 100,000 |
| **EMG** | Skeletal muscle fibres | 50 µV – 5 mV | 20 – 2000 Hz | 1000 |
| **EOG** | Eye movement dipole | 50 – 3500 µV | DC – 100 Hz | 1000 |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (INA) — The Workhorse

The **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** is the universal building block of every bio-amplifier because it provides:
- Symmetric high-impedance differential inputs.
- One external resistor (Rgain) for gain setting.
- Excellent CMRR (>100 dB) without precision matching.

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology (Stage-by-Stage)

**Stage 1 — Buffering + Gain Stage:**
Two op-amps (A1, A2) form a **symmetric differential buffer**. The gain is set by a single resistor Rg between their inverting inputs, while the input signal is applied to the non-inverting inputs (V1 and V2).

**Stage 2 — Difference Amplifier:**
A third op-amp (A3) converts the differential output of Stage 1 into a single-ended output referenced to ground, providing common-mode rejection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Board Favourite Block:** Any question on "design a bio-amplifier" almost always expects you to draw this INA topology and label the gain formula $A = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.

---

## 2.2 The Right-Leg Driver (RLD) Circuit — Patient Safety Subsystem

Because the body acts as a common-mode voltage source (picking up 50/60 Hz from nearby wiring), the **Right-Leg Driver (RLD)** actively drives the patient's right leg with an inverted, attenuated version of the common-mode voltage. This:

1. Reduces the common-mode voltage on the body from several volts to < 5 mV.
2. Improves effective CMRR by 20–40 dB.
3. Provides a safe, current-limited path (typically ≤ 10 µA) to ground.

**Output equation:**

$$V_{RLD} = -\frac{R_f}{R_a} \cdot V_{CM}$$

A current-limiting resistor (typically 1 MΩ) and protection diodes clamp the output in case of defibrillation or fault.

---

## 2.3 Electrical Isolation — Breaking the Ground Loop

Patient-side circuitry must have **no galvanic path** to mains earth. This is achieved using:
- **Isolation Amplifier (e.g., ISO124, AD210):** Transformer or capacitive isolation barrier rated 1500–5000 V RMS.
- **Optical Isolation:** LED-photodiode pair across the barrier.
- **Battery Power:** The patient module is often battery-powered for true galvanic isolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Justification:** A ground fault (e.g., 230 V mains touching the patient cable) without isolation would cause **microshock** (currents > 10 µA passing through the heart are lethal).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXACT formula set examiners expect. Memorize it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | INA Differential Gain | $A_d = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | dimensionless | Sets overall amplification |
| 2 | Common-Mode Gain | $A_c = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} \cdot \dfrac{A_d}{4}$ | dimensionless | Ideally zero |
| 3 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_c \vert}\right)$ | dB | Must be ≥ 80 dB |
| 4 | CMRR with Mismatch | $CMRR \approx \dfrac{A_d}{A_c} = \dfrac{1 + R_3/R_2}{4\Delta R/R}$ | ratio (linear) | Resistor tolerance matters |
| 5 | Noise (RTI) | $v_n = \sqrt{4kTR \cdot \Delta f + e_n^2 \Delta f}$ | V/√Hz | Thermal + op-amp noise |
| 6 | Input Impedance (Bipolar) | $Z_{in} \approx \beta \cdot r_o$ | Ω | 10–100 MΩ typical |
| 7 | RLD Common-Mode Reduction | $V_{CM,new} = \dfrac{V_{CM}}{1 + A_{RLD}}$ | V | Active cancellation |
| 8 | -3 dB Bandwidth | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz | High-pass / low-pass cutoff |
| 9 | Notch Rejection Depth | $A_{notch} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{2\zeta}\right)$ | dB | Q-factor dependent |
| 10 | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_{skin} \approx \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_d} \parallel R_s$ | Ω | Dominates at low freq |

> **Constants to remember:**
> Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> Body temperature $T \approx 310$ K
> Standard mains: **50 Hz (India)**, 60 Hz (USA) — be alert in problems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals:** Patient monitors (Philips IntelliVue, GE CARESCAPE) all use multi-channel INAs.
- **Wearable Tech:** Apple Watch ECG, Fitbit PPG — miniaturized, low-power INAs (e.g., ADS1292, MAX30003).
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Require ultra-low-noise INAs (e.g., Intan RHD2000) with sub-µV noise floor.
- **Defibrillator-Protected Amps:** Special protection circuitry (gas-discharge tube + clamping diodes) is mandatory for ECG amps.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Differential Gain of the Three-Op-Amp INA

Let $V_1$ and $V_2$ be the two input signals. The op-amps A1 and A2 share the same feedback network.

**Step 1:** Because both A1 and A2 have negative feedback through R1 and the gain resistor Rg, the voltage across Rg is:

$$V_{Rg} = V_1 - V_2$$

**Step 2:** The current through Rg is:

$$I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 3:** By KCL, the same current flows through both R1 resistors (A1's and A2's feedback resistors):

$$I_{R_1} = I_{Rg} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}$$

**Step 4:** The output of A1 is its non-inverting input ($V_1$) plus the drop across R1:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{R_1} \cdot R_1 = V_1 + \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 5:** Similarly for A2:

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - \frac{R_1}{R_g}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 6:** The differential output of Stage 1:

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) \cdot \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Stage 2 is a standard difference amplifier with gain $R_3/R_2$ (assuming $R_3/R_2 = R_4/R_5$ for symmetry). Its output:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_3}{R_2}(V_{o1} - V_{o2})$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{A_d = \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Design Trick:** Setting $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_5 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and varying $R_g$ alone gives an adjustable gain from 1 to 1000+ — ideal for clinical gain-ranging.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR Due to Resistor Mismatch in the Difference Stage

Assume $R_2 = R$, $R_3 = R + \Delta R$, $R_4 = R$, $R_5 = R - \Delta R$ (worst-case mismatch).

**Step 1:** Apply a common-mode input $V_{cm}$ to both inputs of Stage 2 (i.e., $V_{o1} = V_{o2} = V_{cm}$). The output of the difference stage becomes:

$$V_{out} = V_{o1} \cdot \frac{R_5}{R_4 + R_5} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_3}{R_2}\right) - V_{o2} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting matched values and simplifying (small-signal assumption, $\Delta R \ll R$):

$$V_{out} \approx V_{cm} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Since the differential input is zero for common-mode testing, the common-mode gain is:

$$A_c = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cm}} \approx \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the linear CMRR is:

$$CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R}$$

**Step 5:** In decibels:

$$\boxed{CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** If $A_d = 100$ and we need $CMRR \ge 100$ dB (i.e., ratio $10^5$), then $\Delta R / R \le 10^{-3}$, which means resistor tolerance must be $\le 0.1\%$ — hence precision metal-film resistors are mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Combined Noise Referred to Input (RTI)

The total input-referred noise consists of thermal noise from the source resistance $R_s$ and the op-amp's voltage noise density $e_n$:

**Step 1:** Thermal (Johnson) noise of source resistance:

$$v_{th} = \sqrt{4 k T R_s \Delta f}$$

**Step 2:** Op-amp voltage noise contribution:

$$v_{opa} = e_n \sqrt{\Delta f}$$

**Step 3:** Op-amp current noise contribution (flowing through $R_s$):

$$v_{i} = i_n R_s \sqrt{\Delta f}$$

**Step 4:** Total input-referred noise (assuming independent sources, RSS):

$$\boxed{v_{n,RTI} = \sqrt{4 k T R_s \Delta f + e_n^2 \Delta f + (i_n R_s)^2 \Delta f}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Convention:** Assume $T = 310$ K (body temp), and ECG bandwidth $\Delta f = 150$ Hz unless otherwise stated.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (ECG Amplifier Design)

**Problem:** Design the gain-setting resistor $R_g$ for an ECG bio-amplifier such that a 1 mV peak ECG signal produces a 1 V output. Use $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and Stage-2 gain $= 10$.

**Step 1:** Required overall gain:

$$A_d = \frac{1 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mV}} = 1000$$

**Step 2:** Stage-2 gain is 10, so Stage-1 gain must be:

$$A_1 = \frac{1000}{10} = 100$$

**Step 3:** From $A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}$:

$$100 = 1 + \frac{2 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega}{R_g}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $R_g$:

$$\frac{2 \times 10^4}{R_g} = 99 \implies R_g = \frac{2 \times 10^4}{99} \approx 202 \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Note:** A standard value of **$R_g = 200\ \Omega$** is used, giving slightly higher gain. Always round to nearest E96 value and re-check bandwidth.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bio-Amplifier vs General-Purpose Amplifier

| Parameter | General-Purpose Op-Amp | Bio-Amplifier (Clinical) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Impedance | 1–10 MΩ | ≥ 100 MΩ (FET/CMOS) |
| CMRR | 60–80 dB | ≥ 100 dB |
| Leakage Current | µA range | < 10 µA (IEC 60601) |
| Isolation | None | Mandatory (1500 V) |
| Noise | Wideband, not critical | < 5 µV RTI over signal band |
| Gain Adjustment | External | Front-panel selectable (×10 to ×10,000) |
| Defibrillation Protection | Absent | Gas-tube + zener clamps |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ECG Bio-Amplifier Signal Chain

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal path from patient electrodes to the digital processor, including isolation, RLD, and filtering stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Patient Electrode RA Right Arm] --> IA1
    P2[Patient Electrode LA Left Arm] --> IA1
    P3[Patient Electrode RL Right Leg] --> RLD1
    P1 --> RLD1
    P2 --> RLD1
    IA1[Three OpAmp Instrumentation Amplifier AD620] --> HP1
    HP1[First Order High Pass Filter fc 0.05 Hz] --> NOTCH1
    NOTCH1[50 Hz Twin T Notch Filter Depth 40 dB] --> LP1
    LP1[Second Order Butterworth Low Pass Filter fc 150 Hz] --> GAIN1
    GAIN1[Programmable Gain Stage x10 to x1000] --> ISO1
    ISO1[Isolation Barrier ISO124 Transformer Coupled 1500 V] --> OUT1
    OUT1[Single Ended Output 0 to 5 V to ADC]
    RLD1[Right Leg Driver Inverting Amplifier with 1 MOhm Current Limit] --> P3
    PWR1[Battery Plus Minus 5 V Isolated DC DC] -.-> IA1
    PWR1 -.-> HP1
    PWR1 -.-> NOTCH1
    PWR1 -.-> LP1
    PWR1 -.-> GAIN1
    PWR2[Mains Powered Plus 12 V DC] --> ISO1
    ISO1 -.-> OUT1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Top row:** Patient-side analog path (galvanically isolated).
> - **Middle:** Active signal conditioning chain.
> - **Bottom:** Two separate power domains (isolated patient side vs mains side).
> - **RLD loop:** Forms a feedback path from common-mode node back to the right-leg electrode.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Three-Op-Amp INA Internal Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Input V1 High Impedance] --> A1IN
    V2[Input V2 High Impedance] --> A2IN
    A1IN[Non Inverting Input of A1] --> A1[OpAmp A1 Buffer Plus Gain]
    A2IN[Non Inverting Input of A2] --> A2[OpAmp A2 Buffer Plus Gain]
    Rgain[External Resistor Rg Single Gain Control] --> A1
    Rgain --> A2
    R1A[Feedback Resistor R1] --> A1
    R1B[Feedback Resistor R1] --> A2
    A1 --> VO1
    A2 --> VO2
    VO1[Differential Output Plus] --> A3INP
    VO2[Differential Output Minus] --> A3INM
    A3INP[Non Inverting Input of A3] --> A3[OpAmp A3 Difference Amplifier]
    A3INM[Inverting Input of A3] --> A3
    A3 --> VOUT[Single Ended Output Vout]
    R2[R2] --> A3
    R3[R3] --> A3
    R4[R4] --> A3
    R5[R5] --> A3
```

---

## 4.3 Frequency Response Visualization (ASCII Schematic)

The ECG amplifier's frequency response is the cascade of three filters:

```
Gain (dB)
   |___________________________________________
   |                                            \____
   |                                                 \____
   |  0.05 Hz  HP cutoff         Notch at 50 Hz        \____ LP cutoff 150 Hz
   |__/                                          \___________/
   |  |                                          |           |
   |__|__________________________________________|___________|________
       0.01      0.05    0.5   10    50    100   150        500  Hz
```

- **Below 0.05 Hz:** Steep roll-off (1st-order HP) removes baseline wander.
- **50 Hz:** Deep notch (40–60 dB attenuation) removes mains interference.
- **Above 150 Hz:** Low-pass roll-off removes muscle artifact and RF noise.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**List the essential requirements of a bio-amplifier used for ECG measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per key point):**

1. **High Input Impedance (≥ 10 MΩ):** Prevents loading of the high-impedance electrode-skin interface, preserving signal fidelity.
2. **High CMRR (≥ 80 dB at 50 Hz):** Rejects common-mode mains interference picked up by the patient's body.
3. **Patient Safety (Isolation):** Galvanic isolation (1500 V) and leakage current < 10 µA as per IEC 60601 standard.
4. **Adjustable Gain (100 – 5000):** To accommodate different signal amplitudes (ECG 1 mV, EEG 50 µV).
5. **Low Noise (RTI < 5 µV):** To detect low-amplitude bio-signals without SNR degradation.
6. **Appropriate Bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz for ECG):** To pass signal of interest while rejecting out-of-band noise.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the Right-Leg Driver (RLD) in a bio-amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

The Right-Leg Driver (RLD) is a negative-feedback active circuit that senses the **common-mode voltage** on the patient's body and reinjects an inverted, attenuated version through the right-leg electrode. Its functions are:

1. **Reduces common-mode voltage** on the body from volts to < 5 mV, drastically improving the effective CMRR of the system.
2. **Provides a safe ground return** with current limited to < 10 µA through a 1 MΩ series resistor.
3. **Improves patient safety** by preventing the buildup of dangerous potentials on the body.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the operation of a **three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier** used as a bio-amplifier. Derive its differential gain expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Right-Leg Driver (RLD) circuit** and discuss its importance in ECG measurement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (1 mark):** Draw the standard 3-op-amp INA with two input buffers (A1, A2) sharing Rg, followed by difference amplifier A3. Use labels V1, V2, Vout, R1, Rg, R2, R3, R4, R5.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- A1 and A2 form symmetric non-inverting amplifiers — they provide high input impedance on both inputs.
- The gain is set by a **single external resistor Rg**, which is convenient for clinical gain-ranging.
- A3 is a unity-gain difference amplifier (or with gain $R_3/R_2$) that converts the differential signal to a single-ended output and rejects common-mode voltages.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
Following the steps in Section 3.1:

$$A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

**Design example (optional, for full marks):** For ECG with 1 mV input, choosing $R_3/R_2 = 10$ and $R_1 = 10$ kΩ with required overall gain 1000, we get $R_g \approx 202\ \Omega$.

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Block diagram (2 marks):**
Draw a summer/inverting amplifier with inputs from the two electrode nodes (RA, LA) summed through equal resistors Ra, feedback resistor Rf, output to the right-leg electrode through a 1 MΩ current-limiting resistor, with protection diodes to ±Vsupply.

**Working (3 marks):**
The RLD measures the average (common-mode) voltage:

$$V_{cm} = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$$

It then outputs:

$$V_{RLD} = -\frac{R_f}{R_a} V_{cm}$$

This drives the right-leg electrode to actively cancel the body-borne interference.

**Importance (2 marks):**
- Reduces common-mode voltage from volts to millivolts.
- Increases effective CMRR by 20–40 dB.
- Limits leakage current to < 10 µA, ensuring IEC 60601 compliance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Past Paper Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **CMRR** in a bio-amplifier. Derive the expression for CMRR in a difference amplifier due to resistor mismatch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the working of an **isolation amplifier** used for patient safety in a bio-amplifier system. List the safety standards it must comply with. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Concept (2 marks):**
**CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** is the ability of a differential amplifier to reject signals common to both inputs (e.g., 50 Hz mains interference picked up by the body) while amplifying the differential signal of interest. It is defined as:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

**Derivation (4 marks):**
Following Section 3.2, with resistor mismatch $\Delta R$:

$$A_c \approx \frac{\Delta R}{R}, \quad A_d = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$$

Therefore:

$$CMRR_{linear} = \frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R}$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d \cdot R}{\Delta R}\right)$$

**Design consequence (1 mark):** To achieve 100 dB CMRR with $A_d = 100$, resistor tolerance must be $\le 0.1\%$.

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw isolation amplifier with three sections — (1) Input op-amp with modulator, (2) Isolation barrier (transformer/capacitor) carrying PWM signal, (3) Output demodulator and output op-amp. Label isolation voltage 1500–5000 V RMS.

**Working (3 marks):**
The input signal is first modulated to a high-frequency carrier (e.g., 200 kHz), passed through the isolation barrier by transformer coupling (no galvanic path), and demodulated back to baseband at the output. Power for the input stage is supplied through an isolated DC-DC converter.

**Safety standards (2 marks):**
- **IEC 60601-1:** Patient leakage current < 10 µA (Type CF, applied to heart).
- **AAMI ES1:** US safety standard for electromedical equipment.
- **Defibrillation-proof:** Withstand 5 kV defibrillator pulse without damage.
- **Creepage and clearance distances** as per IEC 60601-1 (8 mm minimum).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **CMRR formula confusion:** Many students write $CMRR = A_d - A_c$ (in dB). The correct form is the **ratio** in dB: $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$. **−2 marks** for this error.
> 2. **Forgetting the Stage-2 gain in INA:** When computing overall gain, students often stop at $\left(1 + 2R_1/R_g\right)$ and forget to multiply by $R_3/R_2$. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **No units in numerical answers:** Always state CMRR in dB, gain as dimensionless, frequency in Hz, voltage in mV/µV.
> 4. **Confusing "isolation amplifier" with "instrumentation amplifier":** They are NOT the same. INA rejects common-mode noise; isolation amp provides galvanic safety. Examiners will deduct **2 marks** for interchanging.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** Partial marks are awarded for intermediate equations — always show the *mathematical* chain, not just the final box.
> 6. **Not specifying safety standards:** Always mention **IEC 60601-1** when discussing patient safety. Examiners reward this phrase.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minite Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Recall — Read 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Bio-amplifier definition** = Differential amp with high Zin, high CMRR, low noise, patient isolation.
- ✅ **Three key specifications** = Zin ≥ 10 MΩ, CMRR ≥ 80 dB, leakage < 10 µA.
- ✅ **Three-Op-Amp INA gain** = $\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$.
- ✅ **CMRR formula** = $20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ in dB.
- ✅ **Resistor mismatch impact** = $CMRR \approx A_d \cdot R / \Delta R$ → tolerance must be ≤ 0.1 % for 100 dB.
- ✅ **Right-Leg Driver** = Active common-mode canceller; reinverts $V_{cm}$ to right-leg electrode.
- ✅ **Isolation amplifier** = Mandatory for IEC 60601; provides 1500 V galvanic barrier via transformer/capacitor.
- ✅ **ECG bandwidth** = 0.05–150 Hz, 50 Hz notch, gain ≈ 1000.
- ✅ **EEG bandwidth** = 0.1–100 Hz, gain ≈ 10,000–100,000 (lower amplitude).
- ✅ **EMG bandwidth** = 20–2000 Hz, gain ≈ 1000.
- ✅ **Noise formula (RTI)** = $\sqrt{4kTR_s \Delta f + e_n^2 \Delta f + (i_n R_s)^2 \Delta f}$.
- ✅ **Patient safety standard** = **IEC 60601-1** (Type CF, < 10 µA leakage, defibrillation-proof).
- ✅ **High-pass cutoff** = $f_c = 1 / (2\pi RC)$ — used to remove baseline wander.
- ✅ **Low-pass cutoff** = $f_c = 1 / (2\pi RC)$ — used to remove HF noise.
- ✅ **Notch depth** = $20 \log_{10}(1/2\zeta)$ — Q-factor dependent.
- ✅ **Common ECG amp ICs** = AD620, INA128, ADS1292, MAX30003.
- ✅ **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; body T ≈ 310 K.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Bio-Amp Specs — "Z-C-L-N-I":**
> **Z**in high, **C**MRR high, **L**eakage low, **N**oise low, **I**solation mandatory.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Line Interference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Power Line Interference # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Power Line Interference (PLI)**, also referred to as **Mains Hum** or **AC Interference**, is the unwanted contamination of a low-level biopotential signal (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, or EOG) by electromagnetic radiation emanating from AC power transmission lines, building wiring, fluorescent lamps, switching power supplies, and adjacent electrical equipment operating at the fundamental mains frequency. In India, Europe, Africa, and most of Asia, the standard AC power frequency is **50 Hz**, while in North America and parts of Japan it is **60 Hz**. Beyond the fundamental, harmonics at **100 Hz, 150 Hz, 200 Hz ...** (for 50 Hz systems) are also superimposed, though the dominant interference is at the fundamental. The amplitude of PLI on a poorly shielded patient cable can range from a few hundred microvolts to **several volts**, which is often **orders of magnitude larger** than the biopotential being measured (typical ECG ≈ **1 mV**, EEG ≈ **50 µV**). This makes PLI the single most pervasive noise source in clinical electrophysiology. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Imagine you are sitting in a quiet hospital ward trying to hear your friend whisper a secret to you (the heart's electrical signal — about 1 mV). Suddenly, a large industrial air-handling unit two floors below starts humming loudly at exactly 50 Hz (the power line interference — often many volts).
