| **Contrast** | C | $\frac{I_{max} - I_{min}}{I_{max} + I_{min}}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Noise** | σ | Random variation in pixel intensity | Standard deviation |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | SNR | $\frac{\text{Mean signal}}{\sigma}$ | Ratio |
| **Modulation Transfer Function** | MTF | Contrast transfer at each spatial frequency | 0 to 1 |
| **Detective Quantum Efficiency** | DQE | $\left(\dfrac{SNR_{out}}{SNR_{in}}\right)^2$ | 0 to 1 |
| **Focal Spot Size** | F | Effective size of X-ray source | mm |
| **Geometric Magnification** | M | $\dfrac{SID}{SOD}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Penumbra (Blur)** | b | $F \cdot \dfrac{SOD}{SOD - 1}$ | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **SID = Source-to-Image Distance; SOD = Source-to-Object Distance.** Increasing SID and decreasing SOD reduces geometric unsharpness (penumbra), improving image sharpness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.2 X-Ray Photon Interaction Mechanisms Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Incident["INCIDENT X-RAY PHOTON (E, h nu)"]
        PH["Photon with Energy E"]
    end
    
    subgraph PE["PHOTOELECTRIC EFFECT (Dominant below 50 keV)"]
        PE1["Photon fully absorbed"]
        PE2["Inner shell electron ejected as photoelectron"]
        PE3["Characteristic photon + Auger electron emitted"]
        PE1 --> PE2
        PE2 --> PE3

    subgraph CS["COMPTON SCATTERING (Dominant 50-150 keV)"]

        CS3["Recoil electron with transferred energy"]
        CS1 --> CS2
        CS1 --> CS3
    end
    
    subgraph RS["RAYLEIGH SCATTERING (Negligible in diagnostic)"]
        RS1["Coherent scattering - no energy transfer"]
        RS2["Photon changes direction only"]
        RS1 --> RS2
    end
    
    PH -->|Below 50 keV| PE
    PH -->|50-150 keV| CS
    PH -->|Below 30 keV| RS
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define Bremsstrahlung radiation. Explain how it differs from characteristic X-ray radiation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bremsstrahlung (Braking Radiation):** When high-velocity electrons emitted from the cathode strike the tungsten target anode, they are **decelerated** by the strong electric field of the target nuclei. The sudden change in velocity (acceleration) causes the electrons to emit electromagnetic radiation across a **continuous spectrum** of wavelengths, ranging from 0 to a minimum value given by the Duane-Hunt law.

**Characteristic Radiation:** This occurs when an incident electron **ejects an inner-shell (K-shell) electron** from the target atom. The vacancy is filled by an outer-shell electron, releasing a photon whose energy equals the difference between the two shell binding energies. This produces **discrete spectral lines** (K-α, K-β) unique to the target material.

**Key Differences:**
| Feature | Bremsstrahlung | Characteristic |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Spectrum | Continuous | Discrete lines |
| Cause | Electron deceleration | Electron shell transition |
| Energy | 0 to $eV$ | Fixed by target material |
| Direction | Polychromatic | Monoenergetic lines |

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the principle of a line focus in an X-ray tube.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **line focus principle** is used in X-ray tube design to achieve a **small effective focal spot** for high image resolution while maintaining a **large actual focal area** on the anode to distribute heat.

**Construction:** The tungsten target anode is beveled at a small angle $\theta$ (typically 7°–20°) relative to the central axis of the electron beam.

**Formula:** The effective focal spot size is given by:
$$F_{effective} = F_{actual} \times \sin(\theta)$$

**For example:** If $F_{actual} = 5$ mm and $\theta = 12°$, then $F_{effective} = 5 \times \sin(12°) = 5 \times 0.208 = 1.04$ mm.

**Significance:** A small effective focal spot provides **high spatial resolution** in the radiograph, while the large actual focal area allows for better **heat dissipation**, preventing anode damage.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Expected Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and working of a modern diagnostic X-ray tube with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the focusing cup, anode angle, and the high-voltage supply. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Beer-Lambert law for X-ray attenuation. An X-ray beam of intensity 5000 photons/s passes through 8 cm of tissue with linear attenuation coefficient 0.25 cm⁻¹. Calculate the transmitted intensity and the half-value thickness. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of X-Ray Tube — 7 Marks**

A modern diagnostic X-ray tube is a **high-vacuum diode** that converts electrical energy into X-ray photons.

**Major Components:**

1. **Cathode (Negative Electrode):** Consists of a **tungsten filament** (helical coil) heated to ~2500°C by a low-voltage transformer (6–12 V, 3–5 A). Electrons are emitted via **thermionic emission**. The focusing cup (molybdenum) concentrates the electron beam onto the focal spot.

2. **Anode (Positive Electrode):** A **tungsten target** (Z=74, melting point 3422°C) embedded in a copper stem. The anode is beveled at 7°–20° to achieve the **line focus effect**. In rotating anode tubes, the anode spins at 3000–10000 RPM to distribute heat.

3. **Glass/Metal Envelope:** Maintains a high vacuum (10⁻⁶ Torr) to prevent electron collision with air molecules.

4. **High-Voltage Supply:** A step-up transformer with rectification provides **30–150 kVp** across the cathode-anode gap, accelerating electrons to ~50% the speed of light.

5. **Cooling System:** Oil or water circulation in the housing removes heat (99% of electron energy is converted to heat, only 1% to X-rays).

**Working:** Electrons emitted from the cathode are accelerated by the high voltage, strike the tungsten target, and produce X-rays via **Bremsstrahlung** (continuous spectrum) and **characteristic radiation** (discrete lines).

**(b) Beer-Lambert Law Derivation + Numerical — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1):**

Starting from $\dfrac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$, integrate from $x=0$ to $x$:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $I_0 = 5000$ photons/s, $x = 8$ cm, $\mu = 0.25$ cm⁻¹

**Step 1: Calculate Transmitted Intensity**

$$I = 5000 \times e^{-(0.25)(8)} = 5000 \times e^{-2}$$

$$I = 5000 \times 0.1353 = 676.66 \text{ photons/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Half-Value Thickness**

$$HVT = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.25} = 2.772 \text{ cm}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the various interaction mechanisms of X-rays with biological tissue. Compare photoelectric effect and Compton scattering with respect to their energy dependence, Z-dependence, and role in image formation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of an X-ray image intensifier used in fluoroscopy. State its advantages over conventional fluoroscopy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interaction Mechanisms — 7 Marks**

The five possible interactions of X-rays with matter are:
1. Photoelectric effect
2. Compton scattering
3. Coherent (Rayleigh) scattering
4. Pair production (irrelevant in diagnostic radiology, requires > 1.022 MeV)
5. Photodisintegration (irrelevant in diagnostic radiology)

**Photoelectric Effect:**
- **Mechanism:** The X-ray photon transfers ALL its energy to a tightly bound K-shell electron, ejecting it as a photoelectron. The photon is completely absorbed.
- **Energy dependence:** $\tau_m \propto 1/E^3$ (decreases rapidly with increasing energy)
- **Z-dependence:** $\tau_m \propto Z^3$ (strongly increases with atomic number)
- **Role in imaging:** Provides **excellent image contrast** between bone (Z_eff ≈ 13.8) and soft tissue (Z_eff ≈ 7.4). Dominant at low energies (< 50 keV).

**Compton Scattering:**
- **Mechanism:** The X-ray photon collides with a loosely bound outer-shell electron, transferring part of its energy. The photon is scattered at an angle $\theta$ with reduced energy.
- **Energy dependence:** $\sigma_m \propto 1/E$ (gradual decrease)
- **Z-dependence:** $\sigma_m$ depends primarily on **electron density** (roughly constant per gram across tissues)
- **Role in imaging:** **Degrades image quality** by adding scatter "fog". The Compton scatter wavelength shift is $\Delta\lambda = 0.00243(1 - \cos\theta)$ nm. Dominant at 50–150 keV.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Photoelectric | Compton |
|---------|--------------|---------|
| Photon fate | Absorbed | Scattered |
| Z-dependence | $Z^3$ | Electron density |
| E-dependence | $1/E^3$ | $1/E$ |
| Imaging role | Contrast | Noise (fog) |
| Energy range | < 50 keV | 50–150 keV |

**(b) X-Ray Image Intensifier — 7 Marks**

**Principle:** An **X-ray image intensifier (XRII)** converts the transmitted X-ray image into a **bright, visible-light image** that can be viewed in real-time, dramatically reducing patient dose compared to conventional fluoroscopy.

**Construction:**
1. **Input Phosphor (CsI):** Converts incident X-ray photons into visible light photons.
2. **Photocathode:** Converts the visible light image into a **photoelectron image**.
3. **Electrostatic Focusing Lenses:** Accelerate and focus the electrons toward the output phosphor, providing **flux gain** (×50) and **minification gain** (×25).
4. **Output Phosphor (ZnCdS):** Converts the focused electron image into a bright, minified visible-light image.

**Total Brightness Gain:** ~1250× (flux gain × minification gain)

**Working:** X-rays from the patient strike the input phosphor, producing light. This light ejects photoelectrons from the photocathode. The electrons are accelerated (~25 kV) and focused onto the small output phosphor, creating a **bright, small image** that is viewed through a TV camera or directly through an optical system.

**Advantages over Conventional Fluoroscopy:**
1. **Reduced patient dose** (~10× lower)
2. **Real-time dynamic imaging** for catheter guidance, GI studies
3. **High brightness** allows viewing in daylight conditions
4. **Compatibility with TV/video recording** for documentation
5. **Image can be digitally processed** in modern systems

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Units in Final Answers:** Always write units (cm⁻¹, eV, nm, photons/s). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Photoelectric vs. Compton Dependencies:** Photoelectric effect depends on **$Z^3/E^3$**, NOT on electron density. Compton depends on **electron density**, NOT $Z^3$. Getting this wrong costs 2-3 marks.
> 
> 3. **Sign Convention in Beer-Lambert:** Always include the **negative sign** in the differential equation step. Marks are lost for writing $\dfrac{dI}{I} = \mu \, dx$ (positive sign).
> 
> 4. **Line Focus Formula:** Some students write $F_{actual} = F_{eff} \times \sin(\theta)$ (inverted). The correct form is $F_{eff} = F_{actual} \times \sin(\theta)$.
> 
> 5. **Half-Value Thickness Confusion:** HVT is the thickness to **halve** the intensity, NOT to reduce to zero. The formula is $HVT = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$.
> 
> 6. **Numerical Errors in Exponents:** When computing $e^{-\mu x}$, always show intermediate steps. A single arithmetic error in $e^{-2}$ (writing 0.018 instead of 0.135) loses full marks.
> 
> 7. **Skipping Block Diagrams:** For 7-mark questions on X-ray tube construction, a **neat labeled diagram is COMPULSORY**. Text-only answers receive at most 5/7 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist — X-Ray Imaging**

**🔑 Definitions to Memorize:**
- ☑ X-ray: Ionizing EM radiation, $\lambda$ = 0.01–10 nm
- ☑ Bremsstrahlung: Continuous spectrum, from electron deceleration
- ☑ Characteristic radiation: Discrete lines, from shell transitions
- ☑ Linear attenuation coefficient ($\mu$): Probability of interaction per unit path length (cm⁻¹)
- ☑ Mass attenuation coefficient ($\mu_m$): $\mu / \rho$ in cm²/g
- ☑ Half-Value Thickness: $HVT = 0.693 / \mu$

**🔑 Formulas to Memorize (Top 5 for KTU):**
- ☑ Beer-Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$
- ☑ Duane-Hunt: $\lambda_{min} = 1240 / V$ (nm)
- ☑ Line focus: $F_{eff} = F_{actual} \cdot \sin(\theta)$
- ☑ Photoelectric: $\tau_m \propto Z^3 / E^3$
- ☑ Compton shift: $\Delta\lambda = 0.00243(1 - \cos\theta)$ nm

**🔑 Components of X-Ray Tube:**
- ☑ Cathode: Tungsten filament + molybdenum focusing cup
- ☑ Anode: Tungsten target on copper stem, angled 7°–20°
- ☑ Vacuum envelope: 10⁻⁶ Torr
- ☑ High-voltage supply: 30–150 kVp
- ☑ Cooling system: Oil/water circulation

**🔑 Image Quality Parameters:**
- ☑ Spatial resolution ↔ Focal spot size (smaller = better)
- ☑ Contrast ↔ kVp selection (lower kVp = higher contrast)
- ☑ Patient dose ↔ mAs product (higher mAs = higher dose)
- ☑ Penumbra (blur) ↔ SID and SOD geometry

**🔑 Modern Systems:**
- ☑ CR (Computed Radiography): Uses photostimulable phosphor plates
- ☑ DR (Digital Radiography): Direct digital detectors (a-Si + CsI or a-Se)
- ☑ CT (Computed Tomography): Rotating X-ray source with array detector
- ☑ Fluoroscopy: Real-time X-ray imaging using image intensifier
- ☑ Mammography: Low kVp (25–35), Mo/Rh targets, breast compression

**🔑 Safety Note for KTU:**
- ☑ **ALARA Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Achievable
- ☑ Lead apron: 0.25–0.5 mm Pb equivalent
- ☑ Thyroid collar, gonadal shielding mandatory
- ☑ Annual dose limit (occupational): 20 mSv/year
- ☑ Public dose limit: 1 mSv/year
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computed Tomography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Computed Tomography (CT)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that uses computer-processed combinations of multiple X-ray projections taken from different angular orientations around the patient to produce cross-sectional (tomographic) images of specific anatomical regions. The technique reconstructs a volumetric map of the **linear attenuation coefficient (μ)** of tissues, which is then converted into standardized **Hounsfield Units (HU)** for clinical interpretation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** CT is fundamentally a *mathematical reconstruction* of attenuation values from line integrals of X-ray absorption, NOT just a "fancier X-ray." Without the reconstruction algorithm (Radon transform / filtered back-projection), no image exists.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliced Bread" Intuition

Imagine a **fresh loaf of bread** that you cannot slice open to see inside. With a conventional X-ray, it's like pressing the entire loaf flat against a window — you only see a single superimposed shadow of all slices at once (everything is muddled together).

**Computed Tomography** is like a *clever detective* who shines a flashlight through the loaf from **hundreds of different angles**, recording the brightness of light on the other side for every angle. A powerful computer then mathematically "unscrambles" these many overlapping shadowgrams to compute exactly how dense each tiny voxel (3D pixel) inside the loaf is. The result: a **perfect cross-sectional slice**, and then stacked slices give a full 3D model of the inside — without ever cutting the bread open.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **X-ray tube voltage** | **80 – 140 kVp** | Higher kVp = greater penetration |
| **Tube current** | **100 – 800 mA** | Controls photon flux / noise |
| **Slice thickness** | **0.5 – 10 mm** | Determines z-axis resolution |
| **Rotation time** | **0.27 – 1.0 s** | Modern scanners sub-second |
| **Water Hounsfield Unit** | **0 HU** (by definition) | Universal reference |
| **Air Hounsfield Unit** | **−1000 HU** (by definition) | Universal reference |
| **Dense bone** | **+700 to +3000 HU** | High attenuation |
| **Lung parenchyma** | **−700 HU (approx.)** | Low attenuation |
| **Fat** | **−50 to −100 HU** | Negative HU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hounsfield Units are LINEAR**, not logarithmic. A difference of 100 HU between two tissues is *not* a 2× difference in physical density — it is a difference in the **attenuation coefficient** scaled to water.

### 1.4 CT vs. Conventional Radiography — The Core Distinction

| Feature | Conventional X-ray | Computed Tomography |
|---|---|---|
| Output | 2-D projection (superimposed) | Cross-sectional slice |
| Contrast mechanism | Difference in μ along ray path | Local μ value at each voxel |
| Scatter rejection | Moderate | Excellent (collimation) |
| Sensitivity | ~5% density difference | ~0.5% density difference |
| Slice overlap | None (one view) | Contiguous slices possible |
| 3D information | No | Yes (via reconstruction) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Principle — Lambert-Beer Law Foundation

When a monochromatic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a homogeneous material of thickness $x$, the transmitted intensity $I$ follows the **Lambert-Beer law**:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (units: cm⁻¹), which depends on:
- The **atomic number (Z)** of the material — dominant at diagnostic energies
- The **physical density (ρ)** of the material
- The **X-ray photon energy (E)** — μ decreases as E increases

For *inhomogeneous* tissue (which is the real case), the path is divided into many infinitesimal elements:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\int \mu(x,y)\,ds}
$$

The line integral $\int \mu(x,y)\,ds$ along a ray is called the **projection** or **ray sum**, and this is what a CT detector actually measures (after logarithmic transformation).

### 2.2 The CT Number (Hounsfield Unit) Formula

The reconstructed $\mu$ value is converted into a **normalized, scaled, integer** for display:

$$
\text{CT Number} = \text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

**Derivation logic:**
- **Step 1:** Subtract $\mu_{\text{water}}$ → removes the dominant baseline, leaving only the *deviation* of the tissue from water.
- **Step 2:** Divide by $\mu_{\text{water}}$ → makes the number *dimensionless* and energy-independent (the ratio is approximately constant across diagnostic kVp).
- **Step 3:** Multiply by 1000 → converts the tiny fractional value into a convenient clinical integer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{water}} \Rightarrow \text{HU} = 0$
> - $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \mu_{\text{air}} \Rightarrow \text{HU} = -1000$
> - **Water always = 0 HU; Air always = −1000 HU.** This is by definition, not measurement.

### 2.3 The Radon Transform & Reconstruction Philosophy

The mathematical foundation of CT is the **Radon transform**, which states that if we know the line integrals of $\mu(x,y)$ along *all* possible lines through a 2-D object, then $\mu(x,y)$ can be **uniquely recovered** by an inverse transform:

$$
\mu(x,y) = -\frac{1}{2\pi^2} \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \int_{\epsilon}^{\infty} \frac{1}{q} \int_{0}^{2\pi} P(\theta, s) \cdot (x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta - s)\,d\theta\,dq
$$

In practice, the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** algorithm is used because the strict inverse Radon transform is computationally intractable. FBP works in two steps:
1. **Filter** each 1-D projection with a high-pass filter (e.g., Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan) to remove the characteristic "1/r" blur of back-projection.
2. **Back-project** (smear back along the ray path) the filtered projection.
3. **Sum** all back-projected views to form the final image.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lambert-Beer transmission | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | $I$ in photons/cm²·s |
| 2 | Inhomogeneous attenuation | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\int \mu\,ds}$ | Integral over ray path |
| 3 | **CT Number / Hounsfield Unit** | $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless, integer |
| 4 | Detector measurement (log) | $P = \ln(I_0 / I) = \int \mu\,ds$ | This is the *projection* |
| 5 | Mass attenuation coefficient | $\mu_m = \mu / \rho$ | cm²/g |
| 6 | Spatial resolution | $\delta \approx 1.22 \cdot \lambda \cdot \dfrac{\text{FSD}}{\text{focal spot size}}$ | Depends on geometry |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}} \cdot e^{-\int \mu\,ds}$ | Quantum-limited |
| 8 | Slice sensitivity profile (SSP) | Gaussian, FWHM = nominal slice thickness | Approximation |
| 9 | Pitch (helical CT) | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Table travel per rotation}}{\text{Beam collimation}}$ | Typical: 0.5 – 1.5 |
| 10 | CTDI (dose) | $\text{CTDI} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T} \int_{-7T}^{+7T} D(z)\,dz$ | mGy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to compute CT numbers, ALWAYS start by stating the definitions of water = 0 HU and air = −1000 HU. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

### 2.5 Why CT Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Image-Guided Radiotherapy (IGRT):** CT-derived electron density maps are used for accurate radiation dose calculation in cancer treatment.
- **Industrial NDT:** The same Radon reconstruction is used in non-destructive testing of aerospace components, pipelines, and turbine blades.
- **Geophysics / Seismic Imaging:** The mathematical kernel (Radon transform) was first used to image the Earth's subsurface — CT is essentially borrowed geophysics.
- **Materials Science:** Micro-CT (μCT) at sub-micron resolution enables failure analysis of composites, batteries, and bone scaffolds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Hounsfield Number from Lambert-Beer Law

**Given:** Two X-ray beams of equal initial intensity $I_0$ pass through (a) a thickness $d$ of pure water and (b) the same thickness $d$ of an unknown tissue.

**Step 1:** Write the transmitted intensities using Lambert-Beer law.

For water:
$$
I_w = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_w d}
$$

For tissue:
$$
I_t = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_t d}
$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of the ratio to obtain the projections.

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_w}\right) = \mu_w d
$$
$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \mu_t d
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract to isolate the *difference* in attenuation.

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_w}\right) = (\mu_t - \mu_w)\,d
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the water projection to make the quantity dimensionless and energy-independent (since the energy dependence of $\mu$ largely cancels in the ratio at diagnostic energies).

$$
\frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = \frac{1}{\mu_w d}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_w}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 5:** Scale by 1000 to obtain the clinically used Hounsfield Unit.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** If HU > 0, the tissue attenuates X-rays *more* than water (e.g., bone, calcifications). If HU < 0, it attenuates *less* (e.g., fat, air, lung). The factor 1000 is a pure convention to give bone (~+1000 HU) and air (-1000 HU) symmetric, readable values.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — CT Number Calculation

**Problem:** A CT scanner measures the following detector counts for a uniform tissue sample: $I_0 = 1{,}000{,}000$ photons and $I = 250{,}000$ photons through a 2 cm thick sample. For a water reference of the same thickness, $I_w = 500{,}000$ photons. Calculate the CT number.

**Step 1 — Tissue projection:**
$$
P_t = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{250{,}000}\right) = \ln(4) \approx 1.3863
$$

**Step 2 — Water projection:**
$$
P_w = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_w}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000}\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931
$$

**Step 3 — Difference (tissue vs water):**
$$
P_t - P_w = 1.3863 - 0.6931 = 0.6931
$$

**Step 4 — Ratio (this is proportional to the CT number):**
$$
\frac{P_t - P_w}{P_w} = \frac{0.6931}{0.6931} = 1.000
$$

**Step 5 — CT Number:**
$$
\text{HU} = 1000 \times 1.000 = +1000 \text{ HU}
$$

**Result:** The tissue has a CT number of **+1000 HU**, which corresponds to **dense cortical bone** (or a calcified lesion). This is the maximum positive end of the clinical Hounsfield scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students divide by the *thickness* $d$ in Step 4. That is WRONG because $d$ cancels out (the same thickness is used for both water and tissue). The CT number is independent of sample thickness.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Tissue Density from CT Number

**Problem:** A patient's liver lesion has a measured CT number of +45 HU at 120 kVp. Given that $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.15$ cm⁻¹ at 120 kVp, calculate (a) the linear attenuation coefficient of the lesion, and (b) compare it to a normal liver (typical HU = +60).

**Step 1 — Re-arrange the HU formula for $\mu_t$:**
$$
\mu_t = \mu_w \left(1 + \frac{\text{HU}}{1000}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\mu_t$ for the lesion:**
$$
\mu_{\text{lesion}} = 0.15 \times \left(1 + \frac{45}{1000}\right) = 0.15 \times 1.045 = 0.15675 \text{ cm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mu_t$ for normal liver:**
$$
\mu_{\text{liver}} = 0.15 \times \left(1 + \frac{60}{1000}\right) = 0.15 \times 1.060 = 0.15900 \text{ cm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Difference:**
$$
\Delta\mu = \mu_{\text{liver}} - \mu_{\text{lesion}} = 0.00225 \text{ cm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage difference:**
$$
\frac{\Delta\mu}{\mu_{\text{liver}}} \times 100 = \frac{0.00225}{0.159} \times 100 \approx 1.41\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Insight:** A +15 HU difference in liver attenuation represents only ~1.4% change in actual physical attenuation, yet CT can resolve it reliably — this is the *density resolution* advantage of CT over conventional radiography (~100× better).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Generations of CT Scanners

| Generation | Source–Detector Motion | Beam Geometry | Scan Time (typical) | Key Innovation | KTU-Relevant Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st (Translate-Rotate)** | Pencil beam, 1 detector; linear traverse + rotate 1° | Pencil | 4 – 5 min | Original Hounsfield design | Extremely slow, motion artifacts |
| **2nd (Translate-Rotate)** | Narrow fan beam, 5–30 detectors | Narrow fan | 20 – 60 s | Multiple detectors per pass | Still has translational motion |
| **3rd (Rotate-Rotate)** | Wide fan beam, 600+ detectors; both rotate together | Wide fan | 1 – 5 s | No translation; ring of detectors | Ring artifacts, expensive detector array |
| **4th (Stationary detectors)** | Rotating source, **stationary** 360° ring of 1000+ detectors | Wide fan | 1 – 5 s | Detector ring is fixed | Scattered radiation from stationary detectors |
| **5th / Electron-beam CT** | Electron beam steered onto tungsten anodes; no mechanical motion | Fan | 50 ms (per slice) | Ultrafast cardiac imaging | Limited z-coverage, rare today |
| **Helical / Spiral** | Continuous rotation + continuous table translation | Helical path | Sub-second per slice | Slip-ring technology | Slightly reduced z-resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Explain with a diagram the 3rd generation CT scanner and state its advantage over 2nd generation." This is asked in nearly every KTU exam cycle. Memorize the rotate-rotate geometry and the *no translation* advantage.

### 3.5 Helical CT — The Pitch Equation

In helical (spiral) CT, the patient table moves continuously while the X-ray tube rotates. The **pitch** is the key parameter:

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table travel per 360° rotation (mm)}}{\text{Total beam collimation (mm)}}
$$

- **Pitch = 1:** Table travels one collimation width per rotation (standard).
- **Pitch < 1:** Overlapping slices (better z-resolution, higher dose).
- **Pitch > 1:** Gaps in coverage (faster, lower dose, lower z-resolution).
- **Pitch = 1.5:** Typical chest CT for breath-hold protocols.

The **effective slice thickness** in helical CT is slightly larger than the collimated beam width due to the helical path, and is related to pitch by:

$$
\text{Effective slice thickness} \approx \text{Collimation} \times \sqrt{1 + \text{pitch}^2 / k^2}
$$

where $k$ is a reconstruction-algorithm dependent constant.

### 3.6 Image Reconstruction — Why FBP Needs a Filter

**Naïve back-projection** of projections without filtering produces an image with a characteristic **1/r blurring** (a "star" pattern). Each point in the image is the sum of all projections passing through it, which gives a smeared point spread function of $1/r$ shape.

The Fourier transform of a $1/r$ function is a constant divided by spatial frequency $\nu$. Therefore, **multiplying the projection in the frequency domain by $\vert\nu\vert$** (a "ramp" filter) inverts this blur. This is the essence of the **ramp filter** (Ram-Lak filter), often apodized (windowed) to reduce high-frequency noise (e.g., Shepp-Logan, Cosine, Hamming filters).

```
Step-by-step FBP:
  1. Acquire projection P(θ, s) for each angle θ ∈ {0°, 1°, 2°, ..., 179°}
  2. Take 1-D Fourier transform of P → P̃(θ, ν)
  3. Multiply by |ν| (ramp filter) → P̃_filtered(θ, ν)
  4. Inverse 1-D Fourier transform → P_filtered(θ, s)
  5. Back-project: for each pixel (x, y), sum P_filtered(θ, x cosθ + y sinθ) over all θ
  6. Result: reconstructed image f(x, y)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — CT Scanner Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HV[High Voltage Generator 80 to 140 kVp] --> XT[X-Ray Tube Rotating Anode]
    XT --> COL1[Pre patient Collimator]
    COL1 --> PAT[Patient on Translating Table]
    PAT --> COL2[Post patient Collimator]
    COL2 --> DAQ[Detector Array 600 to 1000 elements]
    DAQ --> DAS[Data Acquisition System ADC]
    DAS --> LOG[Logarithmic Amplifier converts to Projection]
    LOG --> REC[Reconstruction Computer FBP Algorithm]
    REC --> MEM[Image Memory 512x512 to 1024x1024]
    MEM --> DISP[Display Console Window Level Adjustment]
    REC --> ARCH[Archive PACS DICOM]

    CTRL[Console Computer] --> HV
    CTRL --> TBL[Table Position Controller]
    TBL --> PAT
    CTRL --> REC
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- The **HV generator** energizes the **X-ray tube** which produces a fan-shaped beam.
- After the **pre-patient collimator** shapes the beam to the desired slice thickness, the beam passes through the **patient**, where it is differentially attenuated.
- The transmitted photons strike the **detector array** (typically solid-state CdWO₄ or ceramic scintillators coupled to photodiodes in modern systems).
- The **DAS** digitizes the signal; the **log amplifier** mathematically converts the exponential attenuation into a *linear projection value* $P = \int \mu \, ds$ — this linearization is essential for back-projection.
- The **reconstruction computer** runs the FBP algorithm to recover $\mu(x, y)$ at every voxel.
- The **display console** applies window/level to map the 12-bit HU range (–1000 to +3095) onto the 8-bit grayscale of a monitor.
- The **archive** stores images in DICOM format for later retrieval.

### 4.2 Diagram — 3rd Generation CT Geometry (Translate-Rotate Eliminated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROTATING_GANTRY ["ROTATING GANTRY rotates together 360 degrees"]
        SRC[X-Ray Tube] --> |fan beam| COL[Collimator]
        COL --> |through| P[Patient Cross Section]
        P --> |fan beam spreads| DET[Curved Detector Array 600 to 1000 elements]
    end
    DET --> COMPUTER[Reconstruction Computer]
    SRC -.-> |synchronized| DET

    NOTE1[Note: NO linear translation] -.-> ROTATING_GANTRY
    NOTE2[Source and detectors rotate as a rigid unit] -.-> ROTATING_GANTRY
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The **X-ray tube and detector array are mounted on a single rigid frame** that rotates together around the patient.
- There is **no linear translation** (unlike 1st/2nd generation) — this eliminates the slow linear scan phase and reduces scan time from minutes to seconds.
- The **detector array is curved** to maintain equal distance from the focal spot for all elements, minimizing geometric distortion.
- A **slip-ring** (not shown) provides continuous power and data transfer, enabling helical (spiral) scanning in modern 3rd-generation systems.

### 4.3 Diagram — Helical CT Acquisition Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T0[Time t equals 0: Tube at angle 0, Table at z0] --> T1[Time t equals 0.5s: Tube rotated 180 degrees, Table advanced by pitch times collimation]
    T1 --> T2[Time t equals 1.0s: Tube rotated 360 degrees, Table at z0 plus pitch times collimation]
    T2 --> T3[Time t equals 1.5s: Tube rotated 540 degrees, Table at z0 plus 2 times pitch times collimation]
    T3 --> Tn[Time t equals N times 0.5s: Continuous helical path traced through patient volume]
    Tn --> REC[Reconstruction computer interpolates helical data onto planar slices]
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The X-ray tube traces a **helical path** around the patient, not a series of discrete circles.
- Data are acquired *continuously* and the reconstruction computer **interpolates** the helical data to produce planar (axial) slices at desired z-positions.
- This is the basis of modern **multi-detector CT (MDCT)** where 64, 128, 256, or even 320 detector rows allow sub-millimeter isotropic voxels.

### 4.4 Comparison Chart — CT Image Quality Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IQ[CT Image Quality] --> SR[Spatial Resolution]
    IQ --> CR[Contrast Resolution]
    IQ --> N[Noise]
    IQ --> A[Artifacts]

    SR --> SR1[High Resolution Matrix 1024x1024]
    SR --> SR2[Thin Collimation 0.5 mm]
    SR --> SR3[Small Focal Spot]
    SR --> SR4[High Sampling kVp 120 to 140]

    CR --> CR1[Wide Dynamic Range 4096 HU]
    CR --> CR2[Good SNR Dose optimization]
    CR --> CR3[Low kVp for iodine contrast 80 kVp]

    N --> N1[Quantum mAs dependent]
    N --> N2[Electronic detector noise]
    N --> N3[Reconstruction algorithm choice]

    A --> A1[Beam hardening cupping artifact]
    A --> A2[Motion blurring cardiac motion]
    A --> A3[Metal streak artifacts]
    A --> A4[Ring artifacts 3rd gen detector drift]
    A --> A5[Partial volume averaging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of a CT scanner," the minimum expected components are: **X-ray source → patient → detector → ADC → reconstruction computer → display**. Omitting any one of these loses at least 1 mark.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define Hounsfield Unit. Why is water assigned a value of 0 HU?**

**Model Answer:**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU), or CT number, is a normalized dimensionless measure of the linear X-ray attenuation coefficient of a tissue, defined as:

$$
\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

Water is assigned a value of **0 HU by definition** because:
1. It is the **most abundant** and reproducible tissue-like material in the human body.
2. It provides a **stable, universally available reference**, removing dependence on scanner calibration, kVp, and environmental conditions.
3. By making water the zero-point, the formula becomes a *deviation metric* — positive HU means more attenuating than water, negative HU means less attenuating.
4. The linear nature of HU makes it easy to display on a fixed grayscale window.

**(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for justification of water = 0)**

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**List any three differences between 2nd generation and 3rd generation CT scanners.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Parameter | 2nd Generation | 3rd Generation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beam geometry | Narrow fan (~10°) | Wide fan (~30°–50°) |
| 2 | Detector count | 5 – 30 detectors | 600 – 1000+ detectors |
| 3 | Motion | Translate + rotate | Rotate + rotate (no translation) |
| 4 | Scan time | 20 – 60 s per slice | 1 – 5 s per slice |
| 5 | Slip-ring | Not used | Used (enables helical) |

**(3 marks: any three correct differences)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Likely Question]**
**(a)** Explain the basic principle of Computed Tomography with a neat block diagram of the CT scanner. (7 marks)
**(b)** Derive the expression for the Hounsfield Unit (CT number) from the fundamental attenuation law. A tissue gives a CT number of +60 HU. If the linear attenuation coefficient of water at 120 kVp is 0.17 cm⁻¹, calculate the linear attenuation coefficient of the tissue. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Basic Principle of CT:**

Computed Tomography is based on the principle that the **internal structure of an object can be reconstructed from multiple X-ray projections taken at different angular orientations**.

When a narrow beam of X-rays passes through the body, the transmitted intensity $I$ is related to the incident intensity $I_0$ by the **Lambert-Beer law**: $I = I_0 \, e^{-\int \mu \, ds}$.

A CT scanner acquires *many* such projections (typically 1000 per rotation) at angles covering 360°. A computer then uses the **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm** to reconstruct a 2-D map of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x, y)$ at every point in the slice. This map is converted to Hounsfield Units and displayed as a grayscale image.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 — same Mermaid diagram should be redrawn on answer script as a clean labelled block diagram with arrows.)*

The essential components are:
1. **High-voltage generator** (80 – 140 kVp)
2. **X-ray tube** with rotating anode
3. **Pre-patient collimator** (defines slice thickness)
4. **Patient table** (translates in helical mode)
5. **Detector array** (solid-state, 600+ elements)
6. **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** with ADC
7. **Logarithmic amplifier** (linearizes exponential data)
8. **Reconstruction computer** (FBP algorithm)
9. **Display console** (window/level adjustable)
10. **Archive** (PACS / DICOM storage)

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for principle, 2 marks for Lambert-Beer, 3 marks for block diagram (neatness + labels count).

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Derivation of Hounsfield Unit:**

Consider two X-ray beams of equal intensity $I_0$ traversing (i) pure water of thickness $d$ and (ii) the unknown tissue of the same thickness $d$.

By Lambert-Beer law:

$$
I_w = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_w d} \quad \text{and} \quad I_t = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_t d}
$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_w}\right) = \mu_w d \quad \text{and} \quad \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \mu_t d
$$

Subtracting and dividing by the water projection to remove thickness dependence:

$$
\frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = \frac{\ln(I_0/I_t) - \ln(I_0/I_w)}{\ln(I_0/I_w)}
$$

Scaling by 1000 to obtain the clinical CT number:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{HU} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}\;}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $\text{HU} = +60$, $\mu_w = 0.17 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 120 kVp.

Re-arranging the formula:

$$
\mu_t = \mu_w \left(1 + \frac{\text{HU}}{1000}\right)
$$

$$
\mu_t = 0.17 \times \left(1 + \frac{60}{1000}\right) = 0.17 \times 1.060 = 0.1802 \text{ cm}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mu_t = 0.1802 \text{ cm}^{-1}\;}
$$

This value is consistent with **soft tissue** (typical range 0.17 – 0.20 cm⁻¹ at 120 kVp).

> **Mark split:** 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for unit, 1 mark for clinical interpretation.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the generations of CT scanners with neat diagrams. Compare 3rd generation and 4th generation CT scanners. (7 marks)
**(b)** What is helical CT? Define pitch. A patient undergoes a helical chest CT with beam collimation of 20 mm and table travel of 30 mm per rotation. Calculate the pitch and comment on the image quality. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**CT Generations — Evolution:**

1. **1st Generation (1971, Hounsfield):** Pencil beam, single detector. **Translate–rotate** motion (linear scan, then 1° rotate, repeat). Scan time: 4–5 min. Limited to head only.

2. **2nd Generation:** Narrow fan beam (10°), 5–30 detectors. Still **translate–rotate**, but fewer steps needed. Scan time: 20–60 s.

3. **3rd Generation:** Wide fan beam (30°–50°), 600–1000 detectors arranged in a curved array. **Rotate–rotate** only — no linear translation. Scan time: 1–5 s. Uses slip-ring for continuous rotation. *(Draw diagram — see Section 4.2.)*

4. **4th Generation:** Rotating X-ray source, but detectors are arranged in a **complete stationary 360° ring** around the patient. No detector rotation needed. Scan time: 1–5 s. Higher scatter due to stationary detectors.

5. **5th Generation (Electron-beam CT):** No mechanical motion. Electron beam steered magnetically onto tungsten anode rings. Scan time: 50 ms. Used for cardiac imaging.

**Comparison: 3rd vs 4th Generation:**

| Feature | 3rd Generation | 4th Generation |
|---|---|---|
| Detector motion | Rotates with source | Stationary (360° ring) |
| Source motion | Rotates | Rotates |
| Slip-ring needed | Yes | Yes (for source) |
| Scatter rejection | Better (rotating collimator) | Poorer (scatter from all directions hits detectors) |
| Cost | Lower (fewer detectors) | Higher (thousands of detectors) |
| Ring artifacts | Possible (detector drift) | Eliminated |
| Modern use | Basis of most current MDCT | Largely obsolete |

> **Mark split:** 2 marks generations list, 3 marks 3rd-gen diagram, 2 marks comparison table.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Helical CT:**

In **helical (spiral) CT**, the X-ray tube rotates continuously around the patient while the table translates *simultaneously* through the gantry. This produces a **helical (corkscrew) path** of the X-ray beam relative to the patient, allowing rapid acquisition of an entire volume in a single breath-hold.

**Definition of Pitch:**

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table travel per 360° tube rotation (mm)}}{\text{Total beam collimation (mm)}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: Beam collimation = 20 mm, Table travel per rotation = 30 mm.

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{30 \text{ mm}}{20 \text{ mm}} = 1.5
$$

**Comment on Image Quality:**

A pitch of **1.5** is *greater than 1*, meaning the table moves 1.5 times the collimation width per rotation. Consequences:
- **Coverage:** Fast volume coverage — ideal for chest CT in a single breath-hold.
- **Dose:** Reduced patient dose (less overlap, fewer redundant projections).
- **Z-axis resolution:** Slightly degraded compared to pitch = 1, with broadening of the slice sensitivity profile.
- **Artifacts:** Mild helical (windmill) artifacts may appear at high-contrast interfaces; interpolation algorithms can partially compensate.

A pitch of 1.5 is the **standard clinical choice** for routine chest CT, balancing coverage, dose, and resolution. For high-resolution lung or inner-ear studies, pitch = 1 or 0.75 would be preferred.

> **Mark split:** 2 marks for helical concept + diagram, 2 marks for pitch formula, 1 mark for calculation, 2 marks for clinical comment.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the reference for HU.** A bare formula without saying "water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU" loses 1 mark. The examiner explicitly looks for this anchor.
> 2. **Confusing linear attenuation coefficient (μ) with mass attenuation coefficient (μ/ρ).** They are different by a factor of density.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** $\mu$ must be in cm⁻¹, HU is dimensionless, pitch is dimensionless. Lose 1 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Inverting the HU formula.** Writing HU $= 1000 \times \mu_w / (\mu_t - \mu_w)$ is the *most common* algebraic slip. Practice the rearrangement until automatic.
> 5. **Drawing the 3rd generation CT with linear translation.** A pure rotate–rotate geometry is mandatory; showing translate arrows loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing "pitch" in CT with "pitch" in NMR or ultrasound.** In CT, pitch *includes* the factor of table travel — it is not a half-period or a frequency.
> 7. **Omitting the logarithmic amplifier** in the block diagram. This single block is the "linearization" step and is often a dedicated 1-mark item in the valuation key.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ CT = **computerized reconstruction of attenuation maps** from multiple angular X-ray projections — *not* just a 3-D X-ray.
- ✅ Core physical law: **Lambert-Beer** — $I = I_0 \, e^{-\int \mu \, ds}$.
- ✅ **CT number (HU)** is dimensionless, normalized to water, scaled by 1000.
- ✅ **Water = 0 HU; Air = −1000 HU** — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ CT resolves **0.5% density differences** vs. 5% for plain radiography — its core clinical advantage.
- ✅ **3rd generation** = rotate–rotate, no translation, 600+ detectors — the KTU favourite.
- ✅ **4th generation** = stationary detector ring, rotating source — mostly obsolete.
- ✅ **Helical CT** uses slip-ring + continuous table motion; **pitch = table travel / collimation**.
- ✅ Pitch = 1 → standard; Pitch < 1 → better z-resolution, higher dose; Pitch > 1 → faster, lower dose, lower z-resolution.
- ✅ **FBP = Filter (Ram-Lak / Shepp-Logan) + Back-Project + Sum** — the workhorse reconstruction algorithm.
- ✅ FBP needs the **log amplifier** upstream because back-projection is a *linear* operation and cannot directly invert exponentials.
- ✅ **Multi-detector CT (MDCT)** = 64, 128, 256+ detector rows → sub-millimeter isotropic voxels in a single breath-hold.
- ✅ Common artifacts: **beam hardening** (cupping, dark streaks), **motion**, **metal streak**, **ring** (3rd gen), **partial volume**.
- ✅ Clinical CT numbers (memorize): **Bone +700 to +3000; Soft tissue +30 to +80; Fat −50 to −100; Lung −700; Air −1000; Water 0**.
- ✅ Mathematical foundation: **Radon transform** (1917) — CT image is the *inverse Radon transform* of the projections.
- ✅ CT dose unit: **CTDI** (mGy); effective dose: **mSv**; ALARA principle always applies.
- ✅ The detector signal is **log-linearized** before reconstruction — this is the single most important signal-processing step in CT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ultrasound

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ultrasound** refers to mechanical, longitudinal, pressure waves that propagate through a medium at frequencies **above the upper limit of human hearing (typically > 20 kHz)**. In the domain of Biomedical Instrumentation, diagnostic ultrasound is restricted to the frequency range of **1 MHz to 20 MHz**, with therapeutic applications extending up to several GHz.

The energy is transmitted via **alternating compression (high-pressure) and rarefaction (low-pressure) cycles** of the constituent particles of the medium. Unlike electromagnetic waves, ultrasound **requires a physical medium** for propagation and cannot travel through a vacuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ultrasound is classified as a **non-ionizing radiation modality**. It does not break chemical bonds or damage DNA, making it the **safest real-time imaging technique** for obstetrics, cardiology, and abdominal diagnostics.

## 1.2 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound in soft tissue (c) | **1540** | m/s |
| Speed of sound in air | 330 | m/s |
| Speed of sound in bone | 4080 | m/s |
| Speed of sound in fat | 1450 | m/s |
| Diagnostic frequency range | 1 – 20 | MHz |
| Therapeutic frequency range | 0.8 – 3.0 | MHz |
| Wavelength in tissue (at 3.5 MHz) | 0.44 | mm |
| Threshold of human hearing | 20,000 | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Rule of Thumb:** The **average speed of ultrasound in human soft tissue is universally taken as 1540 m/s**. This assumption simplifies all diagnostic depth/time calculations and is the basis for the Round-Trip Time (RTT) equation.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a long tunnel with a flashlight. You flash the light, and the light travels to the opposite wall and bounces back to you. By measuring how long the light took to return, you can calculate the depth of the tunnel.

Now replace the **flashlight with an ultrasound transducer**, the **light beam with high-frequency sound pulses**, and the **opposite wall with internal organ boundaries (tissue interfaces)**. The transducer acts as both the **transmitter (source)** and the **receiver (detector)** of the echo. The time delay between transmission and reception is measured, multiplied by 1540 m/s, divided by 2 (for round-trip), and converted directly into anatomical depth.

> This is the exact principle behind **Pulse-Echo Ultrasonography** — the cornerstone of every ultrasound machine in a hospital.

**Real-world biological examples:**
- **Bats** use ultrasound (20–200 kHz) for navigation (echolocation) — a natural parallel to diagnostic imaging.
- **Submarine SONAR** operates on identical physics but uses lower frequencies (10 kHz) for kilometer-scale ranging.
- **Dolphins** use focused ultrasound bursts (up to 150 kHz) to stun prey, analogous to **High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU)** therapy used to ablate tumors non-invasively.

## 1.4 Nature and Classification of Ultrasound Waves

Ultrasound is fundamentally a **mechanical longitudinal wave** characterized by:

- **Particle motion parallel to wave propagation** (longitudinal/compressional).
- Requires an **elastic medium** with non-zero bulk modulus.
- Propagates as a **sinusoidal pressure variation** $\Delta P$ around ambient pressure $P_0$.
- The relationship between particle displacement, particle velocity, and acoustic pressure is governed by the **wave equation**.

In a homogeneous medium, the wave equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$$

where $p$ is the acoustic pressure, $x$ is position, $t$ is time, and $c$ is the speed of sound.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Although ultrasound is longitudinal in soft tissue, it can convert to **transverse (shear) waves** at the boundary with bone, which propagates at roughly half the longitudinal velocity. This is the basis for **acoustic radiation force imaging (ARFI)** and **elastography**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Physics of Ultrasound Propagation

The following sequence describes the **complete physical chain** that converts an electrical signal into a diagnostic image:

**Step 1 — Electrical Excitation:** A high-voltage, short-duration (typically 0.5–2 µs) RF pulse is applied across a **piezoelectric crystal** (commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate, PZT).

**Step 2 — Mechanical Deformation:** The PZT crystal undergoes **rapid dimensional change** (the **inverse piezoelectric effect**), vibrating at the applied frequency. This vibration is coupled into the body via an acoustic gel (impedance matching layer).

**Step 3 — Wave Propagation:** The vibration propagates as a longitudinal pressure wave through soft tissue at a velocity of **1540 m/s**. Wavelength $\lambda$ relates to frequency $f$ via $\lambda = c / f$.

**Step 4 — Acoustic Interaction:** As the wave encounters tissue interfaces (e.g., liver–kidney boundary), part of the energy is **reflected (specular or scattered)** and the rest is **transmitted/refracted**. Reflection is governed by the **acoustic impedance mismatch**.

**Step 5 — Echo Reception:** Reflected echoes strike the same (or a neighboring) piezoelectric crystal, which now operates in the **direct piezoelectric effect** mode, generating a small electrical voltage proportional to the acoustic pressure of the echo.

**Step 6 — Signal Processing:** The time delay, amplitude, and phase of the returning echoes are processed by the scan converter to form a 2D grayscale (B-mode) image or a spectral Doppler display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Round Trip Matters:** The transducer measures the **time-of-flight** of the echo, not the one-way transit. The factor of **2 in the denominator** in all depth equations is the most common source of student error in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.2 Acoustic Impedance — The Core Physical Property

**Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is the product of the **density of the medium** $\rho$ and the **speed of sound** $c$ in that medium. It quantifies the resistance of a medium to the propagation of an acoustic wave.

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

Units: **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)** or **kg/(m²·s)**.

When an ultrasound wave strikes an interface between two media, the **magnitude of the reflected echo** depends entirely on the **difference in acoustic impedance** between the two tissues. This is the single most important equation in diagnostic ultrasound physics.

## 2.3 Reflection and Transmission Coefficients

At a planar interface between two media of impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, for **normal incidence**:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

$$T = 1 - R = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

where $R$ is the **intensity reflection coefficient** (fraction of incident intensity reflected) and $T$ is the **intensity transmission coefficient**.

- $R = 0$ when $Z_1 = Z_2$ (no interface echo — e.g., homogeneous liver parenchyma).
- $R \to 1$ when $Z_2 \gg Z_1$ (e.g., soft tissue to lung or soft tissue to bone).
- A **larger impedance mismatch** produces a **stronger echo** and a brighter pixel in the image.

## 2.4 Attenuation of Ultrasound in Tissue

As ultrasound propagates, its **intensity decreases exponentially** with depth due to **absorption** (conversion to heat), **scattering** (redirection by small reflectors), and **beam divergence**. The attenuation follows the Beer-Lambert law for acoustics:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$

where $I_0$ is the initial intensity, $I(x)$ is the intensity at depth $x$, and $\alpha$ is the **attenuation coefficient** (in dB/cm or Nepers/m).

In clinical practice, the **half-value layer (HVL)** or the **dB/cm·MHz rule of thumb** is used:
- **Soft tissue: 0.5 – 1.0 dB/cm/MHz**
- **Bone: 5 – 10 dB/cm/MHz**
- **Air (lung): ~40 dB/cm/MHz** (essentially opaque to ultrasound)

The total attenuation along a round-trip path of depth $d$ is:

$$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 2 \cdot \alpha \cdot d \cdot f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Compensation:** Ultrasound scanners apply **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC)** or **Depth-Gain Compensation (DGC)** to amplify echoes from deeper structures, since they have suffered more attenuation. TGC curves are an explicit topic in KTU Module 4.

## 2.5 Doppler Effect in Ultrasound

When ultrasound reflects off a **moving target** (e.g., red blood cells), the returned frequency is shifted by an amount proportional to the target velocity. This is the **Doppler shift**.

For a transducer operating at frequency $f_0$, a wave reflecting off a target moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam axis, produces a Doppler shift:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This equation is the foundation of:
- **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler** — measures blood flow velocity.
- **Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler** — measures flow at a specific sample volume.
- **Color Doppler** — superimposes flow velocity map on B-mode image.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave equation | Wavelength calculation |
| 2 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | Depth from round-trip time | Image scaling, numericals |
| 3 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Acoustic impedance | Reflection coefficient |
| 4 | $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ | Reflection coefficient | Interface echo strength |
| 5 | $T = 1 - R$ | Transmission coefficient | Energy budgeting |
| 6 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | Attenuation (linear) | Depth penetration |
| 7 | $A_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB | TGC design |
| 8 | $\text{Atten.} = 2 \alpha d f$ | Total round-trip dB loss | System design |
| 9 | $\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | Doppler shift | Blood flow velocity |
| 10 | $f_{\text{PRF}} \leq \frac{c}{2 d_{\max}}$ | PRF limit (Nyquist) | Aliasing prevention |
| 11 | $R_{\text{axial}} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{BW}}$ | Axial resolution | Transducer design |
| 12 | $R_{\text{lateral}} = \lambda \cdot \frac{z}{D}$ | Lateral resolution | Beam width analysis |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Obstetric Imaging:** Fetal monitoring, gestational age estimation.
- **Cardiology:** Echocardiography, valve flow assessment, ejection fraction.
- **Vascular Studies:** Stenosis detection, deep vein thrombosis.
- **Emergency Medicine:** FAST scan for internal bleeding (Focused Assessment with Sonography for Trauma).
- **Therapeutics:** HIFU for tumor ablation, lithotripsy for kidney stones, physiotherapy.
- **Surgical Guidance:** Real-time needle biopsy tracking.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reflection and Transmission Coefficients from Acoustic Impedance

**Starting Point:** Consider a longitudinal wave traveling in medium 1 (impedance $Z_1$) striking a planar interface with medium 2 (impedance $Z_2$) at normal incidence. Let the incident pressure amplitude be $P_i$, reflected amplitude $P_r$, and transmitted amplitude $P_t$.

**Step 1 — Continuity of Pressure at the Interface:**
The acoustic pressure must be continuous across the interface (no infinite force), so:

$$P_i + P_r = P_t$$

**Step 2 — Continuity of Particle Velocity:**
The particle velocity component normal to the interface must also be continuous:

$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

The numerator sign change accounts for the fact that the reflected wave travels back into medium 1, while the incident and transmitted waves travel forward.

**Step 3 — Express Transmitted Pressure in Terms of Incident and Reflected:**
Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_i + P_r}{Z_2}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Multiply to Eliminate Denominators:**
Multiplying both sides by $Z_1 Z_2$:

$$Z_2 (P_i - P_r) = Z_1 (P_i + P_r)$$

**Step 5 — Expand and Collect Terms in $P_r$:**
Expanding the right-hand side:

$$Z_2 P_i - Z_2 P_r = Z_1 P_i + Z_1 P_r$$

Rearranging all $P_r$ terms to the left and $P_i$ terms to the right:

$$-Z_2 P_r - Z_1 P_r = Z_1 P_i - Z_2 P_i$$

$$-P_r (Z_1 + Z_2) = P_i (Z_1 - Z_2)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Pressure Reflection Coefficient $r_p$:**

$$r_p = \frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}$$

**Step 7 — Intensity Reflection Coefficient:**
Since acoustic intensity is proportional to the square of pressure divided by impedance, and both reflected and incident waves travel in medium 1:

$$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 8 — Intensity Transmission Coefficient:**
By conservation of energy (assuming lossless interface):

$$T = 1 - R = 1 - \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_1 + Z_2}\right)^2 = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of the two fundamental reflection/transmission formulas.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depth Calculation from Round-Trip Time

**Starting Principle:** Ultrasound pulses are emitted at the skin surface, travel to a tissue interface at depth $d$, and return to the transducer. The total path length is $2d$ and the total time is $t$.

**Step 1 — Express the One-Way Distance:**
The wave travels a total distance $2d$ in time $t$ at constant speed $c$:

$$2d = c \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Depth $d$:**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Standard Soft-Tissue Velocity $c = 1540$ m/s:**

$$d = \frac{1540 \cdot t}{2} = 770 \cdot t \text{ (in meters, with } t \text{ in seconds)}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Engineering Form (microseconds):**
Since $t$ is typically measured in microseconds, and we want depth in centimeters, we apply unit conversion. With $t$ in $\mu$s and $d$ in cm:

$$d \text{ (cm)} = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s} \times t \text{ (s)}}{2} = \frac{1540 \times t \times 10^{-6}}{2} \times 100$$

$$d \text{ (cm)} = \frac{1540}{2} \times 10^{-4} \times t = 770 \times 10^{-4} \times t = 0.077 \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{d \text{ (cm)} = 0.077 \times t \text{ (}\mu\text{s)}}$$

This is the working equation used in every ultrasound scanner's beam-former.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Doppler Frequency Shift for Reflected Wave

**Setup:** A stationary transducer emits a wave of frequency $f_0$ toward a target moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$ to the beam axis. We derive the shift in the reflected wave.

**Step 1 — Frequency Perceived by the Moving Target (Observer):**
In the reference frame of the moving target, the source is effectively approaching at velocity $v \cos\theta$ along the beam direction. By the classical Doppler formula (source stationary, observer moving):

$$f_1 = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

(assuming the target moves **toward** the source; sign changes if moving away).

**Step 2 — Wave Reflected by the Target:**
The target re-radiates the wave at frequency $f_1$ as if it were a moving source. The reflected wave reaching the stationary transducer has frequency:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left(\frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$f_2 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify Assuming $v \ll c$:**
Since blood velocity $v$ is on the order of 0.1–1 m/s and $c = 1540$ m/s, the term $v \cos\theta$ is negligible compared to $c$. Using the approximation $\frac{1+x}{1-x} \approx 1 + 2x$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Define the Doppler Shift:**

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This is the celebrated Doppler ultrasound equation used in all clinical blood-flow instruments.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An ultrasound system operates at $f_0 = 5$ MHz. A pulse is reflected from a tissue interface at depth $d = 4$ cm. Calculate the round-trip time, the wavelength in tissue, and the time between successive pulses if the maximum imaging depth is 20 cm.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Round-Trip Time:**

$$t = \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.04}{1540} = 5.19 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 51.9 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

**Part (b) — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.308 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c) — Maximum Pulse Repetition Period (for 20 cm depth):**

$$T_{\text{PRP}} = \frac{2 d_{\max}}{c} = \frac{2 \times 0.20}{1540} = 2.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 260 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

The corresponding **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** is:

$$f_{\text{PRF}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PRP}}} = \frac{1}{2.6 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.85 \text{ kHz}$$

This is well below the Nyquist limit for typical blood velocities, so no aliasing is expected at these depths.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pulse-Echo Ultrasound Imaging System

The complete signal flow from high-voltage pulse generation to displayed B-mode image is shown below. The diagram follows the canonical architecture used in commercial scanners (GE, Philips, Siemens, Mindray).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Master Clock and Timing Generator] --> B[Pulser and TX Beamformer]
    B --> C[TDM Switch Matrix]
    C --> D[Multi-Element Piezoelectric Array Transducer]
    D --> E[Patient Body with Tissue Interfaces]
    E --> D
    D --> F[RX Preamplifier and TGC Amplifier]
    F --> G[RX Beamformer and Time Delay Unit]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector and Log Compression]
    H --> I[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    I --> J[Digital Scan Converter DSC]
    J --> K[Video Processor and Frame Buffer]
    K --> L[Grayscale Display Monitor]
    J --> M[Doppler Processor]
    M --> N[Spectral Display or Color Map Overlay]
```

**Key Signal Flow Description:**

- **A → B:** The master clock generates the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)** triggers. The pulser produces a 50–200 V, 0.5–2 µs RF pulse.
- **B → C → D:** The TX beamformer applies precise **time delays** across array elements to electronically steer and focus the beam. The TDM (Time-Division Multiplexer) routes the high-voltage pulse to the active element.
- **D ↔ E:** The transducer converts electrical → acoustic energy on transmit, and acoustic → electrical on receive. The body acts as a **delay line and acoustic filter**.
- **D → F:** Returning echoes (microvolts level) are pre-amplified (40–60 dB) and gain-compensated by **TGC** to correct for depth-dependent attenuation.
- **F → G:** The RX beamformer applies **dynamic receive focusing**, adjusting delays in real time as echoes arrive from progressively deeper regions.
- **G → H → I:** Envelope detection (rectify + low-pass filter) extracts the amplitude envelope. Log compression (~40 dB dynamic range → 8-bit display) maps the wide amplitude range to displayable grayscale.
- **I → J → L:** The DSC stores echo amplitudes in correct spatial (x, y) coordinates, then displays them as a 2D grayscale B-mode image.
- **J → M → N:** The same RF data is sent to the Doppler processor for spectral or color-flow display.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Ultrasound Transducer Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Backing Material - Damping Block] --> B[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT]
    B --> C[Acoustic Matching Layer 1]
    C --> D[Acoustic Matching Layer 2]
    D --> E[Acoustic Lens or Curved Array Element]
    E --> F[Patient Skin with Coupling Gel]
    B --> G[Electrodes Top and Bottom]
    G --> H[Coaxial Cable to Scanner]
```

**Function of Each Layer (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Backing Material (A):** High-impedance, highly attenuative (e.g., tungsten-epoxy composite). Absorbs backward-radiated energy, **broadens the frequency bandwidth**, and **shortens the pulse length** (improving axial resolution).
- **Piezoelectric Crystal (B):** Active element, typically PZT-5H. Thickness determines resonant frequency: $f_0 = c_{\text{PZT}} / (2 t)$.
- **Matching Layer(s) (C, D):** Quarter-wavelength transformer. Acoustic impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{skin}}}$ minimizes reflection at the crystal-skin interface, maximizing energy transfer.
- **Acoustic Lens (E):** Mechanically focuses the beam in the elevation plane (perpendicular to the scan plane).
- **Coupling Gel (F):** Eliminates the air gap (which would reflect 99.9% of the beam due to massive impedance mismatch: $Z_{\text{air}} = 0.0004$ MRayl vs $Z_{\text{skin}} = 1.6$ MRayl).

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Continuous-Wave (CW) Doppler System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator at f0] --> B[Power Amplifier]
    B --> C[Transmitting Transducer]
    C --> D[Moving Blood Cells]
    D --> E[Receiving Transducer]
    E --> F[RF Amplifier and Mixer]
    A --> F
    F --> G[Low-Pass Filter for Audio]
    G --> H[Spectral Analyzer FFT]
    H --> I[Spectrogram Display Time vs Frequency vs Amplitude]
```

This architecture uses **two separate crystals** (one always transmitting, one always receiving), enabling measurement of very high velocities without aliasing — at the cost of **no range resolution** (all depths are interrogated simultaneously).
