# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define acoustic impedance. Explain its significance in ultrasound imaging with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is defined as the product of the density $\rho$ of the medium and the velocity $c$ of sound in that medium.

$$Z = \rho \cdot c \quad \text{(unit: Rayl or kg·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Significance:** Acoustic impedance is the key physical property that determines how much ultrasound is **reflected or transmitted** at the boundary between two tissues. The **intensity reflection coefficient** is given by:

$$R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Example:** At the **fat–muscle interface**, $Z_{\text{fat}} \approx 1.38 \times 10^6$ Rayl and $Z_{\text{muscle}} \approx 1.70 \times 10^6$ Rayl. The mismatch produces $R \approx 1.1\%$, generating a detectable echo that delineates tissue boundaries in the image. A larger mismatch (e.g., soft tissue vs bone with $Z_{\text{bone}} \approx 7.8 \times 10^6$ Rayl) produces $R \approx 41\%$ — a very strong echo that completely shadows deeper structures.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a pulse-echo ultrasound imaging system.**

**Model Answer:**

A pulse-echo ultrasound system operates on the principle of **time-of-flight measurement**. The block diagram consists of the following stages:

1. **Pulser and Transmitter:** Generates short, high-voltage RF electrical pulses (typically 0.5–2 µs duration, 50–200 V amplitude) at a Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) of 1–10 kHz.
2. **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Converts electrical pulses into acoustic pulses via the **inverse piezoelectric effect** and transmits them into the body.
3. **Propagation and Reflection:** Acoustic pulses travel through tissue at 1540 m/s. At each tissue interface (different acoustic impedance), a portion of the energy is reflected back as echoes.
4. **Reception and Preamplification:** The same transducer (in receive mode) converts returning echoes back into electrical signals using the **direct piezoelectric effect**. These are pre-amplified.
5. **Time-Gain Compensation (TGC):** Amplifies echoes from deeper structures to compensate for attenuation losses.
6. **Signal Processing and Display:** Echoes are processed, scan-converted, and displayed as grayscale pixels on a monitor, with depth proportional to round-trip time and brightness proportional to echo amplitude.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Students often forget to mention the **factor of 2** in the depth equation. Always write $d = ct/2$, not $d = ct$. Losing this 1 mark is one of the most common deductions in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Derive the expression for the intensity reflection coefficient at a tissue interface. Compute the percentage of intensity reflected when ultrasound travels from liver ($Z_1 = 1.66 \times 10^6$ Rayl) to kidney ($Z_2 = 1.62 \times 10^6$ Rayl).** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the piezoelectric effect. Describe the construction and working of an ultrasound transducer with a neat diagram.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Derivation of Intensity Reflection Coefficient:**

At a planar interface between two tissues with acoustic impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, the pressure amplitudes must satisfy **continuity of pressure** and **continuity of particle velocity** at the boundary:

$$P_i + P_r = P_t$$

$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$

Substituting $P_t = P_i + P_r$ into the second equation:

$$\frac{P_i - P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_i + P_r}{Z_2}$$

Cross-multiplying by $Z_1 Z_2$:

$$Z_2 (P_i - P_r) = Z_1 (P_i + P_r)$$

$$Z_2 P_i - Z_2 P_r = Z_1 P_i + Z_1 P_r$$

$$P_i (Z_2 - Z_1) = P_r (Z_1 + Z_2)$$

$$\frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$

Since intensity is proportional to the square of the pressure amplitude (for waves in the same medium):

$$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \left(\frac{P_r}{P_i}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $Z_1 = 1.66 \times 10^6$ Rayl (liver) and $Z_2 = 1.62 \times 10^6$ Rayl (kidney):

$$R = \left(\frac{1.62 - 1.66}{1.62 + 1.66}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{-0.04}{3.28}\right)^2 = (-0.0122)^2 = 1.49 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{R = 0.0149\% \approx 0.015\%}$$

**Interpretation:** This very small reflection (the two tissues have nearly identical impedances) explains why the liver–kidney interface is often **difficult to identify** on B-mode ultrasound and may require contrast agents for clear delineation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Piezoelectric Effect:**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystalline materials (e.g., quartz, PZT, PVDF) to convert **mechanical stress into electrical charge** (direct effect, used for echo reception) and conversely, to undergo **mechanical deformation when an electric field is applied** (inverse effect, used for pulse transmission). Pierre and Jacques Curie discovered the effect in 1880.

**Construction of an Ultrasound Transducer:**

The transducer assembly consists of the following layers (from back to front):

1. **Backing Material (Damping Block):** Made of tungsten-epoxy or similar highly attenuative composite. Its role is to absorb backward-radiated acoustic energy, **broaden the bandwidth**, and shorten the pulse for better **axial resolution**.

2. **Piezoelectric Crystal (Active Element):** A disc or rectangle of PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), typically 0.2–1 mm thick. Thickness $t$ determines the resonant frequency:

$$f_0 = \frac{c_{\text{PZT}}}{2t}$$

3. **Electrodes:** Thin metal films (silver) deposited on both faces of the crystal to apply the RF voltage.

4. **Matching Layer(s):** One or more quarter-wavelength layers with impedance $Z_m = \sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{skin}}}$. They minimize the impedance mismatch and maximize energy transfer into the body.

5. **Acoustic Lens:** A plastic lens bonded to the matching layer that focuses the beam in the **elevation plane** (perpendicular to the scan plane).

6. **Housing and Cable:** The assembly is encased in a metal/plastic shell with a coaxial cable for electrical connection.

**Working:**
- **Transmit Mode:** A high-voltage RF pulse applied to the electrodes causes the crystal to vibrate at the resonant frequency (inverse piezoelectric effect), launching an acoustic pulse into the body.
- **Receive Mode:** Returning echoes mechanically deform the crystal, generating a measurable voltage across the electrodes (direct piezoelectric effect). This is amplified and processed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the Doppler effect in ultrasound. Derive the Doppler shift equation and calculate the Doppler shift for a 5 MHz beam incident on blood flowing at 0.5 m/s at an angle of 30° to the beam axis. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s in blood.** (7 marks)

**(b) Describe the different modes of ultrasound imaging: A-mode, B-mode, and M-mode. List the clinical applications of each.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Concept of Doppler Effect in Ultrasound:**

The Doppler effect refers to the **change in observed frequency** of a wave when there is **relative motion** between the source, the observer, and the reflecting medium. In diagnostic ultrasound, the transducer acts as both transmitter and stationary receiver, while **red blood cells (RBCs)** act as moving scatterers. The shift in the reflected frequency provides a measure of blood flow velocity.

**Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation:**

Step 1: The wave from the stationary transducer of frequency $f_0$ reaches a target moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$. The component of target velocity along the beam axis is $v \cos\theta$. The frequency perceived by the moving target (observer) is:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left(\frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

(Using the classical Doppler formula for a moving observer and stationary source.)

Step 2: The moving target re-radiates the wave as a moving source. The frequency reaching the stationary transducer is:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left(\frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta}\right)$$

Step 3: Substituting the expression for $f_1$:

$$f_2 = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \cdot \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}$$

Step 4: Since $v \ll c$ (typical blood velocity is < 1.5 m/s vs $c = 1540$ m/s), we apply the binomial approximation:

$$\frac{1 + x}{1 - x} \approx 1 + 2x \quad \text{for } x \ll 1$$

where $x = v \cos\theta / c$:

$$f_2 \approx f_0 \left(1 + \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c}\right)$$

Step 5: The Doppler shift is the difference between the received and transmitted frequencies:

$$\Delta f = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $f_0 = 5$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^6$ Hz, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\theta = 30°$, $c = 1540$ m/s.

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 \times \cos 30°}{1540}$$

$$\cos 30° = 0.866$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 \times 0.866}{1540} = \frac{4.33 \times 10^6}{1540}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta f \approx 2812 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.81 \text{ kHz}}$$

This shift lies comfortably in the audible range, which is why Doppler ultrasound instruments use **loudspeakers or headphones** to audibly represent blood flow.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Modes of Ultrasound Imaging:**

**1. A-Mode (Amplitude Mode):**
- The earliest and simplest form of ultrasound imaging.
- The transducer emits a single pulse along one line of sight.
- Returning echoes are displayed as **vertical spikes** on an oscilloscope, with the **x-axis representing depth** (proportional to time) and the **y-axis representing echo amplitude**.
- One-dimensional display.
- **Clinical Applications:** Ophthalmic biometry (axial length of the eye), echoencephalography (midline shift detection in brain), and historically for abdominal measurements. Largely replaced by B-mode in modern practice.

**2. B-Mode (Brightness Mode):**
- The most widely used ultrasound imaging mode today.
- The beam is **electronically scanned** (linear, convex, or phased array) to interrogate multiple lines of sight across a 2D plane.
- Echo amplitude is encoded as **brightness (grayscale)**: strong echoes → white, weak echoes → dark, no echo (anechoic fluid) → black.
- The 2D cross-sectional image is built in real time from hundreds of scan lines per frame.
- **Clinical Applications:** Obstetric imaging, abdominal scanning, cardiac imaging (echocardiography), vascular studies, musculoskeletal imaging, and emergency FAST scans.

**3. M-Mode (Motion Mode):**
- A single scan line is held stationary, and echoes are displayed as a function of time.
- The y-axis represents **depth** and the x-axis represents **time**.
- Stationary structures appear as **horizontal lines**; moving structures (e.g., heart valves) produce **wavy patterns**.
- Provides **excellent temporal resolution** (up to 1000+ frames/s conceptually, since only one line is scanned).
- **Clinical Applications:** Cardiac valve motion analysis, fetal heart rate measurement, and any application requiring high-speed motion capture along a single line.

**Comparison Table (For KTU Answer Script):**

| Feature | A-Mode | B-Mode | M-Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Display type | Spike plot | 2D grayscale | Time-motion plot |
| Dimensions | 1D | 2D | 1D + time |
| Clinical use | Eye biometry | General imaging | Cardiac motion |
| Real-time | No | Yes | Yes |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Part B (14-Mark Questions):**
>
> 1. **Missing "2" in depth formula:** Always use $d = ct/2$ in any ultrasound numerical. The round-trip factor is checked first.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Doppler numerical:** State $f_0$ in Hz, $v$ in m/s, $c$ in m/s to get $\Delta f$ in Hz. KTU examiners explicitly look for unit consistency.
> 3. **Confusing pressure vs intensity reflection coefficients:** The pressure coefficient is $\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ (linear, can be negative). The intensity coefficient is the **square** of this, and is **always non-negative**. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Omitting the matching layer explanation:** The function of the **quarter-wave matching layer** in the transducer construction carries 1–2 marks in KTU board exams. Many students skip it.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption $v \ll c$ in the Doppler derivation:** This is the **key approximation step** that simplifies the binomial expression. Skipping it loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points — Read Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue is ALWAYS 1540 m/s** unless otherwise specified.
- ✅ **Depth formula:** $d = ct/2$ — never forget the factor of 2.
- ✅ **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$, unit is Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).
- ✅ **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** $R = \left(\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}\right)^2$ — note the square.
- ✅ **Attenuation rule of thumb:** ~0.5–1 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue. Total round-trip loss = $2 \alpha d f$.
- ✅ **Doppler shift:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ — derived assuming $v \ll c$.
- ✅ **Diagnostic frequency range:** 1–20 MHz. Higher frequency = better resolution, but shallower penetration.
- ✅ **Axial resolution:** $\dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \text{BW}}$ where BW is the fractional bandwidth of the transducer.
- ✅ **Lateral resolution:** Depends on beam width; improves with focusing and higher frequency.
- ✅ **PRF limit (Nyquist for Doppler):** $f_{\text{PRF}} \leq \dfrac{c}{2 d_{\max}}$ — must satisfy to avoid range ambiguity.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric material:** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate) is the most common.
- ✅ **Coupling gel is mandatory** — air-tissue interface reflects 99.9% of ultrasound.
- ✅ **Backing material** in the transducer broadens bandwidth and improves axial resolution.
- ✅ **Matching layer** has impedance $\sqrt{Z_{\text{PZT}} \cdot Z_{\text{skin}}}$ (quarter-wave transformer).
- ✅ **A-mode** = amplitude spikes; **B-mode** = 2D brightness image; **M-mode** = motion over time.
- ✅ **CW Doppler** has no range ambiguity but no depth selectivity; **PW Doppler** has depth selectivity but is subject to aliasing.
- ✅ **HIFU** = High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound — therapeutic use for tumor ablation and lithotripsy.
- ✅ **Thermal Index (TI)** and **Mechanical Index (MI)** are the FDA safety parameters — keep TI < 1.0 and MI < 1.9 for obstetric scanning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT522 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Resonance Imaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — MAGNETIC RESONANCE IMAGING (MRI)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-invasive, non-ionizing diagnostic imaging modality that exploits the quantum mechanical property of **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen protons (primarily in water and fat) to generate high-resolution, multi-planar tomographic images of anatomical structures, physiological function, and pathological tissues. A strong static magnetic field ($B_0$) aligns the proton spins, a Radio-Frequency (RF) pulse at the **Larmor frequency** tips the net magnetization vector into the transverse plane, and spatially varying gradient fields encode positional information that is decoded via the **Fourier Transform** into the image domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PEICT522 Module 4**
> MRI is assessed under the broader module covering *Medical Imaging Systems* and is heavily weighted (typically 12–15% of ESE marks). Key examinable sub-topics include: NMR principle, Larmor frequency, $T_1$ and $T_2$ relaxation, spin-echo/gradient-echo pulse sequences, MRI system architecture (main magnet, gradient coils, RF coils), k-space and image reconstruction, and MR safety.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Compass in a Magnetic Field"

Imagine thousands of tiny spinning tops (protons) randomly wobbling in your body. When you walk into an MRI room, a powerful invisible magnet (the **main magnet**, $B_0$) lines them all up, like compasses pointing north. Then a brief **radio "shout"** at a precise pitch (the Larmor frequency) tips them all sideways. When the shout stops, they wobble back upright, each one humming a slightly different "note" depending on *where* in the body it is. A sensitive microphone (**RF receiver coil**) listens to this hum, and a mathematical "translator" (**Fourier Transform**) decodes the hums into a detailed cross-sectional picture of the body. Different tissues (fat, water, muscle, tumor) hum at slightly different pitches because of their local chemical environment, which is why MRI can distinguish between healthy and diseased tissue without any X-rays.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen:** $\gamma = 42.58$ **MHz/T**
- **Larmor frequency** at **1.5 T** clinical scanner: $\omega_0 = 63.87$ **MHz**
- **Larmor frequency** at **3.0 T** clinical scanner: $\omega_0 = 127.73$ **MHz**
- **Standard clinical field strengths:** **0.5 T, 1.5 T, 3.0 T** (research scanners up to **9.4 T**)
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Body temperature:** $T \approx 310$ **K** (37 °C)
- **Typical $T_1$ values at 1.5 T:** CSF ≈ 4000 ms, Fat ≈ 250 ms, White Matter ≈ 780 ms, Muscle ≈ 870 ms
- **Typical $T_2$ values at 1.5 T:** CSF ≈ 2000 ms, Fat ≈ 80 ms, White Matter ≈ 90 ms, Muscle ≈ 45 ms

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hydrogen (¹H)?** The human body is ~63% hydrogen by atom count, and hydrogen has the highest gyromagnetic ratio among biologically abundant nuclei, making it the most sensitive nucleus for clinical MRI. ¹³C, ³¹P, ²³Na, and ¹⁹F are used in research/spectroscopy.

### 1.4 The Net Magnetization Vector — Precession

When placed in $B_0$, protons precess (wobble) about the field axis like a spinning top precessing under gravity. The precession angular frequency is the **Larmor frequency**:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio. The populations of parallel (low energy) and anti-parallel (high energy) spins differ only slightly, governed by the **Boltzmann distribution**:

$$\frac{N_{\text{anti-parallel}}}{N_{\text{parallel}}} = e^{-\Delta E / kT} = e^{-\hbar \gamma B_0 / kT}$$

This small population excess ($\sim$ few ppm at 1.5 T) creates the macroscopic **net magnetization vector** $M_0$ that is what we actually measure in MRI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Model Convention:** Conventionally, $B_0$ aligns along the **z-axis** (longitudinal, head-to-foot in the bore). $M_0$ precesses in the **xy-plane** after RF excitation. The transverse component $M_{xy}$ is what the receiver coil detects (it induces an emf via Faraday's law).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of an MRI Scan — Five Logical Phases

1. **Magnetization Alignment (Polarization Phase)**
   - Patient is placed inside the bore of the superconducting magnet ($B_0 = 0.5$–$3.0$ T).
   - Proton spins precess around $B_0$ at $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.
   - Net magnetization $M_0$ builds up along +z, governed by the time constant $T_1$ (longitudinal recovery).
   - Duration: $\sim 3$–$5 \times T_1$ for full equilibrium.

2. **RF Excitation (Resonance Phase)**
   - An RF pulse at $\omega_0$ is transmitted by the **body coil** (or local transmit coil).
   - Pulse is applied along x'-axis (in the rotating frame), tipping $M_0$ by a **flip angle** $\alpha$.
   - Common flip angles: 90° (saturation recovery, spin echo) or <90° (gradient echo, fast sequences).
   - The pulse duration is the **"time-bandwidth product"** that determines slice profile.

3. **Spatial Encoding (Gradient Phase)**
   - **Slice-select gradient** $G_{ss}$ applied simultaneously with RF → selects a 2D slice.
   - **Phase-encoding gradient** $G_{pe}$ → imparts position-dependent phase shifts.
   - **Frequency-encoding (readout) gradient** $G_{ro}$ → imparts position-dependent frequency.
   - Each combination of ($G_{pe}$, $G_{ro}$) fills one line of **k-space**.

4. **Signal Acquisition (Free Induction Decay / Echo Phase)**
   - Transverse magnetization $M_{xy}$ induces an oscillating emf in the **receiver coil**.
   - Raw signal: **Free Induction Decay (FID)** or a refocused **echo** (spin echo / gradient echo).
   - Signal decays with time constant $T_2^*$ (FID) or $T_2$ (echo).

5. **Image Reconstruction (Decoding Phase)**
   - Inverse 2D/3D **Fourier Transform** of the k-space data yields the spatial image.
   - **Pixel intensity** $\propto$ proton density $\times f(T_1, T_2)$ × diffusion × flow × contrast-agent effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MRI is "non-ionizing" but still energetic:** The RF pulses deposit energy in tissue (governed by the **Specific Absorption Rate, SAR**, in W/kg), but the photon energy ($E = h f \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-7}$ eV at 64 MHz) is $\sim 10^{9}$ times smaller than a diagnostic X-ray photon. Therefore, MRI carries no ionizing-radiation risk, but does carry **RF heating** and **peripheral nerve stimulation** risks.

## 2.2 Why the Larmor Frequency is the Heart of MRI

**Physical Origin:** A magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ experiences a torque $\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B}$, which (combined with angular momentum $\vec{J}$) gives the equation of motion:

$$\frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$$

The solution is **Larmor precession** at angular frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$, which in Hz is:

$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} B_0$$

For hydrogen: $f_0$ (MHz) $= 42.58 \times B_0$ (T). This is the **resonance condition** that must be matched by the RF pulse for energy absorption (and therefore signal generation) to occur. Different nuclei have different $\gamma$ values, hence different Larmor frequencies at the same $B_0$ — this is why **MR spectroscopy** can distinguish chemical species.

## 2.3 Relaxation Times — The Tissue Signature

### Longitudinal ($T_1$) Relaxation — "Spin-Lattice"
After RF tipping, the longitudinal magnetization recovers:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t / T_1} \right)$$

**Mechanism:** Energy transfer from excited spins to the surrounding molecular lattice (thermal environment). Most efficient when molecular tumbling rates $\tau_c$ match the Larmor frequency ($\omega_0 \tau_c \approx 1$).

**Tissue contrast:** $T_1$ increases with field strength. Fat has *short* $T_1$ (bright on $T_1$-weighted images). CSF has *long* $T_1$ (dark on $T_1$-weighted images).

### Transverse ($T_2$) Relaxation — "Spin-Spin"
After RF tipping, the transverse magnetization decays due to dephasing:

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \, e^{-t / T_2}$$

**Mechanism:** Local magnetic field fluctuations from neighboring spins cause **dephasing** (no net energy loss, just loss of phase coherence). $T_2 \leq T_1$ always.

**Observable decay $T_2^*$:** Inhomogeneities in $B_0$ accelerate dephasing:

$$\frac{1}{T_2^*} = \frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_2'}$$

where $T_2'$ captures static field inhomogeneity. **Spin echo** sequences use a 180° refocusing pulse to recover $T_2$ information by rephasing spins (canceling $\Delta B_0$ effects).

## 2.4 Bloch Equations — The Master Equations of MRI

The full time-evolution of the magnetization vector $\vec{M} = (M_x, M_y, M_z)$ in a magnetic field with relaxation is given by the **Bloch equations**:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M_y B_z - M_z B_y) - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (M_z B_x - M_x B_z) - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (M_x B_y - M_y B_x) - \frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

In the rotating frame at $\omega_0$ with effective field $\vec{B}_{\text{eff}} = (B_1, 0, B_0 - \omega_0 / \gamma)$, the equations simplify considerably. This rotating-frame formalism is the bedrock of all pulse-sequence design.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Larmor frequency | $f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Hz (or MHz) | Determines RF transmitter frequency |
| 2 | Larmor angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | rad/s | Used in rotating frame analysis |
| 3 | Energy gap of proton spin | $\Delta E = \hbar \gamma B_0$ | J | NMR resonance energy |
| 4 | Boltzmann population ratio | $\frac{N_-}{N_+} = e^{-\hbar \gamma B_0 / kT}$ | dimensionless | Determines available signal $M_0$ |
| 5 | Longitudinal recovery | $M_z(t) = M_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right)$ | A/m | $T_1$-weighted contrast |
| 6 | Transverse decay | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 e^{-t/T_2}$ | A/m | $T_2$-weighted contrast |
| 7 | Effective transverse decay | $\frac{1}{T_2^*} = \frac{1}{T_2} + \gamma \Delta B_0$ | s$^{-1}$ | Gradient-echo signal envelope |
| 8 | Flip angle | $\alpha = \gamma B_1 \tau_{\text{pulse}}$ | rad | RF pulse design |
| 9 | Slice thickness | $\Delta z = \frac{\text{BW}_{\text{RF}}}{\gamma G_{ss}}$ | m | Slice-select gradient design |
| 10 | Field-of-View (FOV) | $\text{FOV} = \frac{1}{\Delta k} = \frac{2\pi}{\gamma G_{ro} \Delta t}$ | m | Readout gradient design |
| 11 | Pixel resolution | $\Delta x = \frac{\text{FOV}_x}{N_x}$ | m | Matrix size trade-off |
| 12 | Ernst angle (optimal flip) | $\cos \alpha_E = e^{-TR/T_1}$ | rad | Maximizes SNR in FLASH-type sequences |
| 13 | SNR scaling with field | $\text{SNR} \propto B_0^{1.5\text{–}2}$ | — | Drives push to 3 T, 7 T |
| 14 | Specific Absorption Rate | $\text{SAR} \propto B_0^2 \alpha^2 \cdot f_{\text{RF}}$ | W/kg | Patient safety limit (FDA: 4 W/kg whole-body avg) |
| 15 | Spatial encoding frequency | $\omega(x) = \gamma (B_0 + G_{ro} x)$ | rad/s | Linear position-to-frequency mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory trick for KTU board exams:** "$T_1$ is **T**ime to align with **B**oltzmann (longitudinal recovery), $T_2$ is **T**ime to **d**ephase (transverse decay)." Always remember $T_2 \leq T_1$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MRI principles you've just learned power:
- **Functional MRI (fMRI):** Detects Blood-Oxygen-Level-Dependent (BOLD) signal — the basis of all modern neuroscience brain-mapping studies.
- **Diffusion-Weighted Imaging (DWI):** Maps water diffusion in tissue; gold standard for acute stroke detection (within minutes of onset).
- **MR Angiography (MRA):** Visualizes blood vessels without contrast agents.
- **Cardiac MRI:** Quantifies ejection fraction, wall motion, and myocardial viability.
- **MR Spectroscopy (MRS):** Detects metabolites (NAA, Cho, Cr) for tumor grading.
- **Interventional MRI:** Real-time image guidance for neurosurgery and biopsy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Larmor Frequency from the Bloch Equation

We start from the **equation of motion for a magnetic moment in a magnetic field** (classical mechanics, applicable because the expectation value of a spin obeys classical equations):

$$\frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$$

**Step 1:** Consider only the static field along z, so $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$.

Substitute into the cross product:

$$\vec{M} \times \vec{B} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ M_x & M_y & M_z \\ 0 & 0 & B_0 \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the determinant to get the cross-product components:

$$= \hat{x}(M_y B_0 - 0) - \hat{y}(M_x B_0 - 0) + \hat{z}(0 - 0)$$

$$= (M_y B_0) \hat{x} - (M_x B_0) \hat{y}$$

**Step 3:** Write the three component equations of motion:

$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma M_y B_0$$

$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = -\gamma M_x B_0$$

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate the first equation with respect to time, then substitute the second:

$$\frac{d^2 M_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma B_0 (-\gamma B_0 M_x) = -(\gamma B_0)^2 M_x$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this as the equation of a simple harmonic oscillator:

$$\frac{d^2 M_x}{dt^2} + \omega_0^2 M_x = 0 \quad \text{where} \quad \omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

**Step 6:** The general solution is sinusoidal precession. Define the angular frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to frequency in Hz by dividing by $2\pi$:

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

**Step 8:** Numerical evaluation for hydrogen at 1.5 T:

$$f_0 = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8 \, \text{rad/T·s})(1.5 \, \text{T})}{2\pi}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{4.0125 \times 10^8}{6.2832} \approx 63.87 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz} = 63.87 \, \text{MHz}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 1.5 T clinical scanner transmits RF at $\sim$64 MHz, which is in the **VHF band** (analogous to TV channel 3). This is why the scanner room must be a **Faraday cage**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $T_1$ Recovery Curve

**Step 1:** From the Bloch equation for the z-component with only $B_0$ present:

$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = -\frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

**Step 2:** This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearrange to separate variables:

$$\frac{dM_z}{M_z - M_0} = -\frac{dt}{T_1}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate both sides. Let $u = M_z - M_0$, then $du = dM_z$:

$$\int \frac{du}{u} = -\int \frac{dt}{T_1}$$

$$\ln \vert M_z - M_0 \vert = -\frac{t}{T_1} + C$$

**Step 4:** Apply the initial condition: at $t = 0$, $M_z = M_z(0) = M_0 \cos\alpha$ (after a flip angle $\alpha$):

$$\ln \vert M_0 \cos\alpha - M_0 \vert = C \implies C = \ln(M_0 \cos\alpha - M_0) = \ln(M_0(\cos\alpha - 1))$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$M_z - M_0 = M_0 (\cos\alpha - 1) e^{-t/T_1}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $M_z(t)$:

$$\boxed{M_z(t) = M_0 \left[ 1 - (1 - \cos\alpha) e^{-t/T_1} \right]}$$

**Step 7:** Special case: for a 90° pulse ($\alpha = \pi/2$, $\cos\alpha = 0$):

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left[ 1 - e^{-t/T_1} \right]$$

This is the **classic $T_1$ recovery curve** — verified against the standard MRI physics literature.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Spin Echo vs. Gradient Echo

| Parameter | Spin Echo (SE) | Gradient Echo (GRE) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Refocusing mechanism | 180° RF pulse | Gradient polarity reversal |
| Flip angle | 90° | Variable (<90°, Ernst angle) |
| Sensitive to | $T_2$ (true) | $T_2^*$ (includes $\Delta B_0$) |
| Susceptibility artifact | Low (refocuses $\Delta B_0$) | High |
| Scan time | Longer (TR typically long) | Shorter (small flip, short TR) |
| Typical use | Anatomical $T_1$/$T_2$ weighting | Fast 3D, dynamic imaging, fMRI |
| SAR | Higher (more RF power) | Lower |
| Flow sensitivity | Can be black-blood or bright-blood | Time-of-flight MRA |

**KTU Exam Trap:** Many students confuse $T_2$ and $T_2^*$. Always remember that **spin echo** gives you **true $T_2$** because the 180° pulse rephases spins dephased by static field inhomogeneities. **Gradient echo** gives you **$T_2^*$** which is *faster* but *less specific* to tissue properties.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the 1D Fourier Encoding Relation

**Step 1:** The Larmor frequency at position $x$ in the presence of a readout gradient $G_{ro}$ is:

$$\omega(x) = \gamma(B_0 + G_{ro} x)$$

**Step 2:** The accumulated phase of a spin at position $x$ over readout time $t$ is:

$$\phi(x, t) = \int_0^t \omega(x) \, dt' = \gamma \int_0^t (B_0 + G_{ro} x) \, dt'$$

**Step 3:** The signal $S(t)$ measured by the coil is the integral (sum) over all spins in the sample, weighted by proton density $\rho(x)$:

$$S(t) = \int \rho(x) e^{i\phi(x,t)} dx = \int \rho(x) e^{i(\gamma B_0 t + \gamma G_{ro} x t)} dx$$

**Step 4:** Demodulate at $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ (remove carrier):

$$S(t) = \int \rho(x) e^{i \gamma G_{ro} x t} dx$$

**Step 5:** Define the **k-space coordinate** $k_x = \frac{\gamma G_{ro} t}{2\pi}$:

$$S(k_x) = \int \rho(x) e^{i 2\pi k_x x} dx$$

**Step 6:** Recognize this as the **forward Fourier Transform** of $\rho(x)$. By the Fourier inversion theorem:

$$\rho(x) = \int S(k_x) e^{-i 2\pi k_x x} dk_x$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the *fundamental theorem of MRI*: traversing k-space (one line per phase-encoding step) and applying the inverse Fourier Transform reconstructs the image. The **readout gradient** $G_{ro}$ moves us along the $k_x$ axis at speed $\gamma G_{ro} / 2\pi$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A 1.5 T MRI scanner uses a slice-select gradient of 5 mT/m and an RF pulse bandwidth of 2 kHz. Calculate (a) the slice thickness, and (b) the Larmor frequency.

**Solution:**

(a) Slice thickness:

$$\Delta z = \frac{\text{BW}_{\text{RF}}}{\gamma G_{ss}}$$

$$\Delta z = \frac{2000 \, \text{Hz}}{(42.58 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz/T})(5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{T/m})}$$

$$\Delta z = \frac{2000}{2.129 \times 10^5} \approx 9.39 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} \approx 9.4 \, \text{mm}$$

(b) Larmor frequency at 1.5 T:

$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \, \text{MHz}$$

**Answer:** Slice thickness $\approx 9.4$ mm, Larmor frequency $= 63.87$ MHz. Note that bandwidth and gradient are inversely related to slice thickness — clinical protocols use gradients of 20–40 mT/m for sub-millimeter slices.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MRI System Block Diagram — Major Subsystems

The complete MRI scanner is shown below as a high-level block diagram mapping the major subsystems and their functional relationships. This is the KTU-recommended schematic for examination purposes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Patient Table and Bore] --> B[Superconducting Main Magnet B0]
    A --> C[Gradient Coil Assembly X Y Z]
    A --> D[RF Body Coil Transmit]
    A --> E[RF Receiver Coil Local]
    B --> F[Cryocooler and Helium Reservoir]
    F --> B
    C --> G[Gradient Amplifier Cabinet]
    G --> C
    D --> H[RF Transmitter and Synthesizer]
    H --> D
    E --> I[Receiver Preamp and ADC]
    I --> J[Pulse Sequence Controller]
    J --> K[Reconstruction Computer GPU Cluster]
    K --> L[Image Storage PACS]
    L --> M[Display Workstation]
    M --> N[Radiologist Console]
    J --> D
    J --> C
    J --> I
    O[Faraday Shielded Room] --> A
    P[Chiller and Gradient Cooling] --> C
```

**Subsystem Functions (mapped to KTU exam rubric):**
- **Superconducting Main Magnet ($B_0$):** Generates the static field (0.5–3 T). Wound with NbTi wire, cooled to 4 K in liquid helium bath.
- **Gradient Coil Assembly:** Three orthogonal (X, Y, Z) coils produce spatially linear magnetic field gradients for slice selection, phase encoding, and frequency encoding.
- **RF Body Coil:** Transmit coil that radiates the $B_1$ field at $\omega_0$. In modern scanners, a phased-array local coil serves as both transmit and receive.
- **RF Receiver Coil:** Detects the FID/echo signal (Faraday induction from precessing $M_{xy}$).
- **Pulse Sequence Controller:** The "conductor" — orchestrates timing of RF pulses, gradient switching, and signal acquisition.
- **Reconstruction Computer:** Applies Inverse Fourier Transform to k-space data to produce images (real-time with GPU acceleration).
- **Faraday Shielded Room:** Contains the ~64 MHz RF, prevents external interference, and isolates the scanner from public RF bands.

---

## 4.2 MRI Pulse Sequence Timing Diagram — Spin Echo

The diagram below shows the canonical Spin Echo (SE) sequence as a function of time — this is the most commonly tested timing diagram in KTU 2024 scheme exams.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[t equals 0 ms RF 90 deg pulse] --> B[Gradient Gss slice select ON]
    B --> C[TE equals 2 ms dephasing gradient Gss rewinder OFF]
    C --> D[TE equals 10 ms RF 180 deg refocusing pulse]
    D --> E[TE equals 20 ms readout gradient Gro ON and signal acquisition]
    E --> F[TR equals 500 ms repeat cycle]
    F --> A
    G[Phase encode Gpe pulsed between each TR] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU key points):**
- **TE (Echo Time):** Time between 90° pulse and the peak of the echo. Longer TE → more $T_2$ weighting.
- **TR (Repetition Time):** Time between successive 90° pulses. Longer TR → less $T_1$ weighting; shorter TR → strong $T_1$ weighting.
- **Slice-select gradient ($G_{ss}$):** ON during RF pulses only.
- **Phase-encoding gradient ($G_{pe}$):** Different amplitude each TR — fills k-space line by line.
- **Readout gradient ($G_{ro}$):** ON during signal acquisition; reverses polarity to form the echo.

---

## 4.3 k-Space Trajectory — 2D Cartesian Sampling

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[k-space origin ky equals 0 kx equals 0] --> B[Line 1 kx sweep with ky equals delta ky]
    B --> C[Line 2 kx sweep with ky equals 2 delta ky]
    C --> D[Line 3 kx sweep with ky equals 3 delta ky]
    D --> E[Continue for N phase encode steps]
    E --> F[Full k-space matrix Nx by Ny]
    F --> G[Inverse 2D Fourier Transform]
    G --> H[Final Image in x and y spatial domain]
```

**Key KTU exam points:**
- Each line of k-space is acquired in one TR period.
- The **center of k-space** ($k_x = k_y = 0$) contains the **low-spatial-frequency** information — this is where **contrast** lives.
- The **edges of k-space** contain **high-spatial-frequency** information — this is where **edges and fine detail** live.
- Truncating k-space (faster scan) reduces resolution; this is the basis of **parallel imaging** (SENSE, GRAPPA).
- Non-Cartesian trajectories (radial, spiral, PROPELLER) are used in research for motion-robust imaging.

---

## 4.4 MRI Signal Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Proton Spins in Body] --> B[Net Magnetization M0 precesses at Larmor]
    B --> C[RF Pulse at Larmor Frequency]
    C --> D[Transverse Magnetization Mxy]
    D --> E[Receiver Coil Faraday Induction]
    E --> F[Low Noise Preamplifier]
    F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    G --> H[Digital Receiver and Demodulation]
    H --> I[k-space Data Buffer]
    I --> J[2D Inverse FFT]
    J --> K[Image Magnitude and Phase]
    K --> L[DICOM PACS Storage]
```

This block diagram maps the complete signal pathway from spin physics to a clinical DICOM image. KTU examiners frequently ask: "Trace the signal from patient to image."

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEICT522]`
**Define Larmor frequency. State the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Larmor frequency** is the natural precession frequency of a magnetic moment (such as a proton's spin) in an external static magnetic field. Mathematically:
$$f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$
where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio of the nucleus and $B_0$ is the applied field strength.

For **hydrogen-1 protons** ($\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T) in a 1.5 T scanner:
$$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$
**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer.)**

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEICT522]`
**Differentiate between $T_1$ and $T_2$ relaxation processes in MRI. Which one is responsible for $T_1$-weighted image contrast?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $T_1$ (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice) | $T_2$ (Transverse / Spin-Spin) |
|--------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Physical process | Energy transfer from spins to surrounding lattice (thermal reservoir) | Dephasing of spins due to local field fluctuations; no net energy loss |
| Recovery/decay | $M_z$ recovers toward $M_0$ | $M_{xy}$ decays toward 0 |
| Time constant | $T_1$ (longer) | $T_2 \leq T_1$ |
| Image contrast | **$T_1$-weighted contrast uses short TR and short TE** | $T_2$-weighted contrast uses long TR and long TE |

**$T_1$** is responsible for $T_1$-weighted image contrast. **(1 mark each for differentiation in 2 aspects + 1 mark for identifying $T_1$ for $T_1$-weighted contrast.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an MRI scanner. Explain the role of the superconducting main magnet, gradient coils, and RF coils. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Larmor frequency relation from the Bloch equation. A 3 T MRI scanner is used — calculate the Larmor frequency for hydrogen and discuss its implications for RF coil design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — MRI System Architecture (7 marks)

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). The major subsystems are:

1. **Superconducting Main Magnet (2 marks)**
   - Generates the homogeneous static field $B_0$ (0.5–3.0 T clinical, up to 9.4 T research).
   - Wound with **NbTi superconducting wire** cooled to **4.2 K** in a liquid-helium cryostat.
   - Field homogeneity $\leq 10$ ppm over a 40-cm spherical volume.
   - Persistent-mode operation (zero-resistance current loop).

2. **Gradient Coils (2 marks)**
   - Three orthogonal (X, Y, Z) sets produce linear field gradients (typically up to 40–80 mT/m, slew rate 150–200 T/m/s).
   - **X-gradient (left-right):** frequency encoding (readout).
   - **Y-gradient (anterior-posterior):** phase encoding.
   - **Z-gradient (head-foot):** slice selection.
   - Driven by high-power gradient amplifiers (~1 MW peak).

3. **RF Coils (2 marks)**
   - **Transmit coil:** radiates $B_1$ field at $\omega_0$ (e.g., 64 MHz at 1.5 T) to tip magnetization. The whole-body birdcage coil is standard.
   - **Receive coil:** detects transverse magnetization via Faraday induction. Local surface coils and phased arrays improve SNR.
   - The coil must be tuned to $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ and matched to 50 $\Omega$.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark)**
   - Integration of all subsystems with the pulse sequence controller and image reconstruction computer is essential. The Faraday-shielded scan room contains RF noise; the cryocooler maintains superconductivity.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Larmor Derivation and 3 T Calculation (7 marks)

**Derivation** (4 marks) — refer to the complete derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps:

- Start with $\frac{d\vec{M}}{dt} = \gamma \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$ for $\vec{B} = B_0 \hat{z}$.
- Evaluate cross product → coupled ODEs in $M_x, M_y$.
- Differentiate and substitute to get a harmonic-oscillator equation.
- Identify the natural frequency:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \implies f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}}$$

**Calculation at 3 T** (2 marks):

$$f_0 = 42.58 \, \text{MHz/T} \times 3 \, \text{T} = 127.74 \, \text{MHz}$$

**Implications for RF coil design** (1 mark):
- The RF coil must be tuned to **127.74 MHz** (VHF band), requiring a different matching network than a 1.5 T scanner.
- **Wavelength** in tissue at 127.74 MHz is $\lambda = c/f \approx 2.3$ m — close to body dimensions, leading to **standing wave** and **RF inhomogeneity** artifacts. This is why 3 T scanners often use **dielectric pads** and **parallel transmit** technology.
- Higher SAR at 3 T (scales as $B_0^2$) limits the number of slices per TR.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of nuclear magnetic resonance. Discuss the role of $T_1$ and $T_2$ relaxation times in producing MR image contrast. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the spin-echo pulse sequence with a timing diagram. Explain how the choice of TR and TE determines whether the resulting image is $T_1$-weighted, $T_2$-weighted, or proton-density-weighted. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — NMR Principle and Relaxation Contrast (7 marks)

**NMR Principle (3 marks):**
- Certain nuclei (e.g., ¹H) possess **non-zero nuclear spin** ($I = 1/2$ for hydrogen), giving them a magnetic dipole moment $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{I}$.
- In an external field $B_0$, the spin can align parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy), with energy gap $\Delta E = \hbar \gamma B_0$.
- A small population excess ($\sim$ few ppm) in the lower state, governed by the Boltzmann factor, produces a **net macroscopic magnetization** $M_0$ along $B_0$.
- Applying an RF pulse at the **Larmor frequency** $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ induces transitions; the net magnetization tips into the transverse plane.
- When the RF is switched off, the magnetization returns to equilibrium by emitting a signal (FID), captured by the receiver coil.

**$T_1$ and $T_2$ in Image Contrast (4 marks):**
- **$T_1$ contrast:** Tissues with short $T_1$ (fat) recover $M_z$ quickly → appear **bright** in $T_1$-weighted images. Tissues with long $T_1$ (CSF, water) appear **dark**. Achieved with **short TR, short TE**.
- **$T_2$ contrast:** Tissues with long $T_2$ (CSF, edema, cysts) retain $M_{xy}$ longer → appear **bright** in $T_2$-weighted images. Achieved with **long TR, long TE**.
- **Clinical use:** $T_1$-weighted → anatomical detail; $T_2$-weighted → pathology (most pathologies increase water content → bright on $T_2$).
- **Proton density (PD) contrast:** Long TR, short TE — minimizes both $T_1$ and $T_2$ effects, so contrast reflects $\rho$ (proton density).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Spin Echo Sequence and Weighting (7 marks)

**Spin Echo Timing Diagram (3 marks)** — refer to Section 4.2 of these notes. Key events:
- **t = 0:** 90° RF pulse (with slice-select gradient) tips $M_0$ into xy-plane.
- **t = TE/2:** 180° RF pulse (slice-select gradient also) refocuses dephased spins.
- **t = TE:** Echo forms (peak signal) — acquired with frequency-encoding (readout) gradient ON.
- **Phase-encoding gradient:** pulsed with a different amplitude each TR.
- **TR:** full cycle time between successive 90° pulses.

**Sequence parameters (4 marks):**

| Weighting | TR (ms) | TE (ms) | Image Appearance |
|-----------|---------|---------|------------------|
| **$T_1$-weighted** | Short (<700) | Short (<30) | Fat bright, CSF dark, good anatomy |
| **$T_2$-weighted** | Long (>2000) | Long (>80) | Water/CSF bright, fat intermediate |
| **PD-weighted** | Long (>2000) | Short (<30) | Intermediate contrast, balanced $T_1$/$T_2$ |

**Explanation of TR/TE choice:**
- **TR controls $T_1$ contrast:** Short TR → incomplete $T_1$ recovery → tissues with short $T_1$ (fat) recover more, become bright. Long TR → near-full recovery → less $T_1$ contrast.
- **TE controls $T_2$ contrast:** Short TE → minimal $T_2$ decay → less $T_2$ contrast. Long TE → significant $T_2$ decay → tissues with long $T_2$ retain signal and appear bright.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in KTU MRI questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_2$ and $T_2^*$:** Spin echo measures true $T_2$; gradient echo measures $T_2^*$. A 1-mark penalty is typical for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Larmor calculation:** Always specify whether the answer is in **Hz, kHz, or MHz**. Many students write "63.87" without units → lose 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Wrong gyromagnetic ratio:** Use $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T for **¹H only**. Other nuclei (³¹P, ¹³C, ²³Na) have different values.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram:** A block diagram is worth 2–3 marks in "explain the MRI system" type questions. Always include one.
> 5. **Not distinguishing $T_1$-weighted from $T_2$-weighted TR/TE:** Memorize the table. Examiners specifically test this in Part B questions.
> 6. **Confusing slice-select, phase-encode, and frequency-encode gradients:** Mnemonic: **S**lice-select is on with **S**ame RF pulse; **P**hase-encoding pulses between TRs; **R**eadout (frequency) is on during acquisition.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention the Faraday cage / cryocooler / safety:** These are KTU 2024 high-weight topics — MR safety is now explicitly in the syllabus.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Must-Know Definitions
- **MRI:** Non-ionizing imaging modality using NMR of hydrogen protons.
- **Larmor frequency:** $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ — the resonance frequency.
- **$T_1$:** Longitudinal (spin-lattice) relaxation time; $M_z$ recovery.
- **$T_2$:** Transverse (spin-spin) relaxation time; $M_{xy}$ decay.
- **$T_2^*$:** Effective transverse decay including $B_0$ inhomogeneity.
- **Net magnetization $M_0$:** Macroscopic sum of proton magnetic moments.
- **Flip angle $\alpha$:** Tip angle of $M_0$ from +z axis after RF pulse.
- **k-space:** Fourier domain; one line per TR; inverse FT → image.
- **TR:** Repetition time (between 90° pulses).
- **TE:** Echo time (between 90° pulse and signal peak).
- **SAR:** Specific Absorption Rate — RF power per kg of tissue (limit: 4 W/kg).

### Must-Know Numerical Values
- $\gamma_{^1H} = 42.58$ MHz/T
- $f_0$ at 1.5 T = 63.87 MHz
- $f_0$ at 3.0 T = 127.74 MHz
- Typical TR range: 5–5000 ms
- Typical TE range: 1–500 ms
- Typical flip angle: 5°–180°
- Slice thickness: 0.5–10 mm
- FOV: 5–50 cm
- Matrix: 128×128 to 1024×1024
- Clinical $T_1$ (1.5 T): Fat ≈ 250 ms, CSF ≈ 4000 ms
- Clinical $T_2$ (1.5 T): Fat ≈ 80 ms, CSF ≈ 2000 ms

### Must-Know Pulse Sequences
- **Spin Echo (SE):** 90°–180° → true $T_2$ imaging; gold standard for $T_2$ weighting.
- **Gradient Echo (GRE):** Variable flip angle → $T_2^*$ weighting; fast, used in fMRI/MRA.
- **Inversion Recovery (IR):** 180°–90°–180° → excellent $T_1$ weighting, used in FLAIR, STIR.
- **Echo Planar Imaging (EPI):** Single-shot ultrafast → fMRI, DWI, perfusion.

### Must-Know Clinical Applications
- **Brain:** Tumors, stroke (DWI gold standard), MS, hemorrhage (SWI).
- **Spine:** Disc herniation, cord lesions, multiple sclerosis plaques.
- **Cardiac:** Viability, ejection fraction, congenital disease.
- **Musculoskeletal:** Ligament tears, meniscal injury, bone marrow edema.
- **Abdomen/Pelvis:** Liver lesions, prostate cancer, gynecological imaging.

### Must-Know Safety Rules
- **Absolute contraindications:** Certain pacemakers, cochlear implants, ferromagnetic aneurysm clips, metallic intraocular foreign bodies.
- **Relative contraindications:** First-trimester pregnancy, claustrophobia, certain implants.
- **FDA limits:** SAR $< 4$ W/kg whole-body average; $\Delta B/dt$ gradient-induced peripheral nerve stimulation limit.
- **Quench:** Rapid helium boil-off can occur accidentally → emergency venting required; never enter the room during a quench.
- **Projectile risk:** Never bring ferromagnetic objects (oxygen tanks, scissors, keys) into Zone IV of the MR suite.

### Quick Mnemonics for the Board Exam
- **"Long T1 dark, Long T2 bright"** — T1-W (CSF dark), T2-W (CSF bright).
- **"Flip is sine, decayed is exponent"** — $\sin\alpha$ for transverse, $e^{-t/T_2}$ for decay.
- **"Short TR, Short TE → T1"**; **"Long TR, Long TE → T2"**.
- **"Center of k-space is contrast, edges are detail"**.
- **"Phase encode pulses between TRs"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In Part A, always give the **formula + numerical value + unit**. In Part B, always start with a **block diagram** or **timing diagram** and then explain. Examiners award 2–3 marks purely for correct, well-labeled diagrams. Memorize the Spin Echo and Gradient Echo parameter tables — at least one Part B question will test the $T_1$/$T_2$/$PD$ weighting logic.
